answer:
Short answer from the research: **yes, “interesting work”/intrinsic motivation is one of the more reliable predictors of job satisfaction and engagement, and usually looks more important than a modest pay increase once people are already financially comfortable.** But “interesting” is not the only intrinsic factor: **autonomy, meaning, learning, social fit, managerial quality, and control over one’s work** matter a lot too. A small-team lead role could satisfy some of those even if the technical domain is less exciting.

## 1. Job content predicts satisfaction more strongly than pay

A large literature on job design finds that people are happier and more engaged when their work has:

- autonomy/control  
- skill variety  
- task significance/meaning  
- feedback  
- learning/challenge  
- good social support  
- fit with their interests and abilities

Classic and meta-analytic work: Hackman & Oldham’s job characteristics model; Fried & Ferris 1987; Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007.

The Humphrey et al. meta-analysis found that motivational job characteristics—autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity—have **moderate to large associations** with job satisfaction and internal motivation, often in the rough range of **r ≈ .3–.5**. Those are large by organizational psychology standards.

By contrast, pay level has a much weaker average relationship with job satisfaction. Judge et al. 2010, a meta-analysis on pay and satisfaction, found that **actual pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction**, around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction/fairness matters more than raw salary, but the broad result is: once basic needs and fairness are met, more money is not a very strong predictor of liking your job.

Important caveat: correlations are not perfectly comparable, and salary may matter a lot if it changes your financial security, family options, immigration situation, ability to buy a house, etc. But for two already high-paying roles, a “bit higher salary” would generally be predicted to have a smaller happiness effect than a large difference in daily work experience.

## 2. Person-job fit and “work I actually care about” are strongly related to satisfaction

A related literature studies **person-job fit**: whether the job matches your skills, interests, values, and preferred work style.

Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005 meta-analysis found that person-job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction—roughly **r ≈ .5** in many estimates—and negatively associated with intent to quit. That is one of the stronger findings in this area.

There is also a vocational-interest-congruence literature, based partly on Holland’s theory of career fit. Meta-analyses such as Assouline & Meir 1987 and Tranberg, Slane & Ekeberg 1993 generally find that people whose work matches their interests are more satisfied, though the effect is more modest than broad person-job fit—often around **r ≈ .2**. So “I find the work interesting” matters, but it is only one component of fit.

## 3. Meaningful work/calling predicts satisfaction, but can be a double-edged sword

Research on meaningful work finds large associations with job satisfaction, engagement, and lower turnover intentions. Allan et al. 2019, a meta-analysis on meaningful work, found meaningful work to be strongly related to job satisfaction and engagement, with correlations often above **r ≈ .5**.

Similarly, “calling” research—Wrzesniewski et al. 1997; Duffy and colleagues—finds that people who experience their work as a calling often report higher job and life satisfaction.

But there is a downside: people in “calling” jobs may tolerate overwork, lower pay, or worse conditions because the work feels important. Bunderson & Thompson 2009, for example, studied zookeepers and described calling as a “double-edged sword”: it gave meaning, but also made people more willing to accept sacrifice.

So the research does not say “always choose the more interesting/meaningful job.” It says meaningful and intrinsically motivating work is valuable, but not if it comes bundled with unsustainable demands, bad management, or unfairness.

## 4. Intrinsic motivation matters especially for complex/creative work

For research/engineering work specifically, intrinsic motivation may matter not only for happiness but also for performance.

Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford 2014, a Psychological Bulletin meta-analysis, found that intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially the **quality** of performance and especially in complex work. Extrinsic incentives also matter, particularly for quantity/effort, but intrinsic motivation is especially relevant for creative, ambiguous, high-skill tasks.

That maps pretty well onto research engineering: if you find the problem genuinely compelling, you may do better work and sustain motivation longer.

## 5. Money does matter, but marginal money has diminishing/heterogeneous effects

On general well-being, the income literature is nuanced:

- Kahneman & Deaton 2010 found that life evaluation rises with income, while day-to-day emotional well-being appeared to plateau around a moderate-high income level in their data.
- Killingsworth 2021 challenged the plateau claim using experience-sampling data, finding well-being continues rising with income.
- Mellers, Kahneman & Killingsworth 2023 reconciled some of this: for many people well-being continues rising with income, but the effect is heterogeneous and roughly logarithmic; for the least happy group, extra income may stop helping much after a point.

Practical implication from the research: **money keeps mattering, but each additional percentage increase tends to matter less than the previous one**, especially if you are already financially secure. A modest raise is unlikely, on average, to compensate for a large worsening in day-to-day work quality.

## 6. Leadership is ambiguous: it can increase autonomy/meaning, but also stress and less hands-on work

The “leading a small team” part should not be treated as merely extrinsic. Leadership can provide:

- autonomy  
- influence  
- mentoring/relatedness  
- a sense of impact  
- career growth  
- status/recognition

Those are psychologically important. Self-determination theory—Deci & Ryan; Gagné & Deci 2005; Van den Broeck et al. 2016—emphasizes autonomy, competence, and relatedness as basic psychological needs at work. A good team lead role can satisfy all three.

But management can also mean:

- less time doing the technical work you find intrinsically rewarding  
- more meetings  
- more coordination and conflict  
- responsibility without full control  
- performance-management stress

The job-demand/control literature, starting with Karasek 1979, suggests that high demands are less harmful when paired with high control. So the key question is not “management or not?” but: **would the Google role give you real autonomy and influence, or just accountability plus meetings?**

## 7. On regret: direct evidence is thinner, but people often overweight salient factors like salary/prestige

There is not, as far as I know, a clean longitudinal literature that asks: “People chose interesting lower-paid job vs boring higher-paid job; who regretted it 10 years later?” Most regret research is broader.

However, affective-forecasting research is relevant. Wilson & Gilbert’s work on affective forecasting and Kahneman et al. 2006 on the “focusing illusion” show that people often overestimate the long-term happiness impact of salient, easy-to-compare features—salary, prestige, location, title—and underweight repeated daily experience.

A related example is the commuting literature. Stutzer & Frey 2008 found evidence for a “commuting paradox”: people accept longer commutes for higher pay or housing advantages, but long commutes remain strongly associated with lower life satisfaction. The interpretation is that people often underpredict the cumulative cost of daily hassles.

There is also research by Whillans and colleagues showing that people who prioritize time over money tend to report higher happiness, and experimental work showing that buying time can increase positive affect. This does not directly answer job choice, but it supports the general idea that **daily lived experience often matters more than people expect when making decisions around money**.

## Bottom-line reading of the evidence

If I translate the literature into your specific tradeoff:

- **A modest salary increase**, by itself, is unlikely to be a large happiness gain if you are already well compensated.
- **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work** is one of the strongest known predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.
- **Person-job fit** is very important. If Anthropic gives you unusually strong fit and Google would not, that is a major point for staying.
- But **leading a small team** may itself be a major source of intrinsic satisfaction if you genuinely want mentoring, ownership, and organizational influence.
- The dangerous option, according to the research, is choosing the Google role mainly because the salary/title are salient, while underweighting what your Tuesday afternoon will feel like six months in.

A research-consistent way to frame it is:

> Do not compare “interesting work” versus “salary.” Compare the full day-to-day job design of each role: autonomy, learning, meaning, people, control, workload, and fit. The evidence suggests those factors will dominate modest compensation differences for long-run job satisfaction.

**Exploring job satisfaction factors**

Yes, people in more meaningful jobs tend to be more satisfied and experience better well-being. While pay and title do matter, their impact diminishes beyond a certain point. Intrinsic rewards, particularly in leadership, can hold value if that's what someone truly values.  

Job content and fit play crucial roles. Research shows that factors like autonomy, skill variety, task significance, and feedback all positively influence job satisfaction and motivation. Person-job fit is among the strongest predictors of satisfaction, highlighting the importance of alignment between roles and personal interests.
**Analyzing work satisfaction**

A 2019 meta-analysis shows that meaningful work is strongly linked to job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions, though these findings are mostly correlational. While having a sense of "calling" can enhance satisfaction, it may also lead to overwork and exploitation. 

Regarding money, a 2010 meta-analysis indicates that pay levels have only a weak correlation with job satisfaction (around 0.15). However, how satisfied someone is with their pay is more impactful, especially in relative terms.
**Considering practical checks**

I think I could include some practical, evidence-based checks for my decisions. For instance, I could compare my weekly calendar and see the balance between research and meetings. Asking Google could clarify my exact scope, autonomy, technical depth, promotion path, and team quality, while turning to Anthropic might help with lead opportunities. Also, it’s important to consider whether the compensation increase is significant enough to really impact my life in a meaningful way.
answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of relevant research, though not usually framed exactly as “interesting AI research job vs higher-paid less-interesting team-lead job.” The closest literatures are job-design research, intrinsic motivation, person–job fit, pay/job-satisfaction research, and life-regret/affective-forecasting research.

The short version is:

> Across many studies, the “content of the work” — autonomy, intellectual challenge, skill use, task significance, meaning, fit with one’s interests — is usually one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and work-related well-being. Pay matters, but its marginal effect is modest once basic security and comfort are already met, especially for already high earners. Leadership can matter a lot if it gives autonomy, growth, impact, and identity — but title/pay alone are weaker predictors than the day-to-day work.

Below are some specific research findings.

---

## 1. Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is strongly linked to job satisfaction

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman & Oldham. It says jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:

- **Skill variety** — using different high-level abilities  
- **Task identity** — owning a whole piece of work  
- **Task significance** — the work matters  
- **Autonomy** — control over how to do the work  
- **Feedback** — clear information about results

A large meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** reviewed decades of job-design research and found that these work-design features have moderate-to-large relationships with job satisfaction, internal motivation, performance, and lower strain. Autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, and social support all matter.

So, in research terms, “interesting work” is not a fluffy preference. It maps onto well-studied predictors of well-being at work.

A related meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** on person–job fit found that fit between a person’s abilities/interests and their job is one of the strongest correlates of job satisfaction and lower turnover intentions. Roughly: people are happier when the job actually uses what they care about and are good at.

**Implication for your case:** if the Anthropic role is much better aligned with your intellectual interests, that is a real predictor of future satisfaction, not merely a luxury consideration.

---

## 2. “Meaningful work” is also strongly associated with well-being

There is a separate literature on meaningful work and “calling.” Meta-analytic work, e.g. **Allan et al., 2019**, finds that meaningful work is strongly associated with:

- higher job satisfaction  
- higher work engagement  
- greater life satisfaction  
- lower withdrawal intentions  
- lower burnout/strain in many contexts

This is especially relevant if “interesting” for you also means “important,” “mission-aligned,” or “I am working on problems I think matter.”

That said, the calling/meaning literature has a caveat: people who see work as a calling can also tolerate worse conditions, overwork, or exploitation. So meaningfulness is good, but it does not make pay, boundaries, or career progression irrelevant.

---

## 3. Pay matters, but less than people often expect — especially at high incomes

A widely cited meta-analysis by **Judge et al., 2010** looked at the relationship between pay and job satisfaction. The relationship between **pay level** and **job satisfaction** was positive but fairly small — around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction is more related to job satisfaction than raw pay, but still not dominant.

In subjective well-being research:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising with income.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** challenged the simple plateau story, finding that experienced well-being continues to rise with income, but roughly logarithmically.
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023** reconciled this: income continues to matter for many people, but the marginal effect is smaller at high incomes and varies by baseline happiness.

The important practical point is the logarithmic/diminishing-returns nature. If you are already highly compensated, a “bit higher salary” is likely to have a smaller average effect on daily happiness than a large change in job content, autonomy, stress, or meaning — unless the extra money materially changes your life: housing, family security, ability to retire earlier, immigration constraints, debt, etc.

**Implication:** if the Google offer is only moderately higher and both jobs already pay enough for financial security, the research would usually predict that day-to-day work quality matters more than the pay delta.

---

## 4. Promotions and leadership are not automatically happiness gains

Leadership could be very good for you — but the research would ask *why* you want it.

A team-lead role can increase:

- autonomy  
- influence  
- status  
- learning  
- mentorship opportunities  
- sense of impact  
- career capital

Those are real sources of satisfaction.

But management also often increases:

- meetings  
- interpersonal conflict  
- responsibility without full control  
- performance-management burden  
- less time doing the technical work itself  
- stress and ambiguity

Research on job demands and control — e.g. the **Karasek job demand–control model** and later job-demands/resources work — suggests that demanding jobs can be energizing when they come with high autonomy/resources, but harmful when demands exceed control.

There is also evidence of “honeymoon–hangover” effects in job changes: people often get a boost after switching jobs or receiving promotions, but some of that boost fades as novelty wears off and daily realities dominate. See work by **Boswell et al.** on job-change honeymoon/hangover patterns.

**Implication:** the key question is not “team lead or not?” but “What would my ordinary Tuesday look like?” If the leadership role is mostly mentoring excellent people on important technical problems, it may be intrinsically rewarding. If it is mostly planning, reporting, alignment meetings, and less interesting product work, the title may not compensate.

---

## 5. People often overweight salient external factors and underweight daily experience

There is a large affective-forecasting literature — **Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman, Schkade**, etc. — showing that people are imperfect at predicting what will make them happy. One relevant pattern is the **focusing illusion**: when comparing options, we overweight salient, easily comparable features.

Salary, company brand, title, and “leading a team” are salient.  
Daily intellectual engagement, meeting load, autonomy, coworkers, and sense of meaning are less salient but often dominate lived experience.

A classic adjacent finding is the **commuting paradox** from **Stutzer & Frey, 2008**: people accept longer commutes for higher income, but the extra income often does not fully compensate for the daily well-being cost. This is not directly about your situation, but it illustrates the general principle: people sometimes trade recurring daily experience for visible rewards and later do not get the happiness payoff they expected.

**Implication:** when evaluating the Google offer, do not just compare salary/title. Compare the repeated lived experience: the problems, people, autonomy, meeting load, pace, and meaning.

---

## 6. Regret research is less direct, but career regrets are common

There is less clean research on “Do people regret choosing interesting work over money?” But some regret research is relevant.

**Roese & Summerville, 2005** reviewed common life regrets and found that education and career are among the most common regret domains. **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** found that, over the long run, regrets of inaction often loom larger than regrets of action.

But this does not straightforwardly mean “take the Google offer.” It means people tend to regret paths where they feel they failed to pursue an important opportunity or failed to act in accordance with their values.

So you could regret either:

- not trying leadership when you had the chance, or  
- leaving unusually meaningful frontier AI research for a less interesting role.

The research does not tell you which regret will be stronger. It suggests that regret is often about self-concordance: “Did I choose the path that matched the kind of person I wanted to become?”

Relevant here is the self-concordance/intrinsic-goals literature: **Sheldon & Elliot**, **Kasser & Ryan**, and **Niemiec, Ryan & Deci** generally find that pursuing intrinsic goals — growth, relationships, contribution, mastery — is more reliably associated with well-being than pursuing extrinsic goals like money, image, or status.

But leadership can be intrinsic if it represents growth, mentorship, responsibility, and impact. It is not automatically extrinsic.

---

## My evidence-based read

Based on the research, I would not treat “interesting work” as a soft factor. It is one of the major predictors of job satisfaction.

If the situation is:

> Anthropic: deeply interesting, meaningful, high-fit work  
> Google: somewhat more money, management title, but substantially less interesting day-to-day work

then the literature would generally predict that staying with the more interesting/high-fit work is more likely to preserve long-term work satisfaction, unless the Google role provides something intrinsically important to you — e.g. real leadership growth, mentoring, autonomy, or impact.

The most research-aligned decision questions are probably:

1. **Will the salary difference materially change my life, or is it mostly a scorekeeping/status difference?**  
2. **Is leading a small team intrinsically meaningful to me, or mainly attractive as a career milestone?**  
3. **What fraction of my week would be spent on work I find absorbing?**  
4. **Would the Google role increase autonomy and impact, or mostly increase coordination burden?**  
5. **Can I get leadership opportunities at Anthropic without giving up the interesting work?**  
6. **Which choice better matches the person/researcher/leader I want to become over the next 5–10 years?**

If “leading a team” is a deep developmental goal, you should take that seriously. But the research would caution against overvaluing the visible package — salary, title, brand — relative to the daily substance of the work.

answer:
Short answer: **yes, the research generally finds that “interesting work” — usually measured as intrinsic motivation, autonomy, skill variety, challenge, learning, task significance, and person–job fit — is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and often spills over into life satisfaction.** Moderate pay differences matter much less once income is already high, though role autonomy, workload, manager quality, social environment, and career identity can matter as much as “interestingness.”

There is less direct research on “do people regret staying in the more interesting job?”, but regret research suggests people most regret **missed opportunities tied to their ideal self** — which could mean either “I left unusually meaningful research” or “I never tried leading a team,” depending on your values.

## 1. Interesting/autonomous work is a major predictor of job satisfaction

A large body of organizational psychology uses the **Job Characteristics Model**: jobs are more motivating when they have autonomy, skill variety, task identity, task significance, and feedback.

Key findings:

- **Hackman & Oldham, 1976; 1980**: classic model linking enriched work to internal motivation and satisfaction.
- **Fried & Ferris, 1987**, meta-analysis: job characteristics reliably correlate with job satisfaction and motivation.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, large meta-analysis: motivational job characteristics — autonomy, task significance, feedback, complexity, skill variety — are strongly associated with job satisfaction, internal motivation, lower turnover intentions, and performance.

The practical takeaway: “interesting work” is not a soft preference. It overlaps with some of the best-established predictors of enjoying work: **autonomy, challenge, growth, feedback, and meaningful contribution**.

## 2. Person–job fit is very important

Another relevant construct is **person–job fit**: how well the work matches your interests, abilities, and needs.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, meta-analysis: person–job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit. Correlations with job satisfaction are often in the roughly `.4–.6` range, which is large by social-science standards.

For your case, the key question is not just “Google vs Anthropic,” but:

> Which role better fits the kind of daily problems I want to solve and the kind of person I want to become?

A less technically interesting job with leadership, mentoring, strategy, and broader leverage could still have high fit if those are genuinely motivating to you. But if the main appeal is salary/status while the day-to-day work feels dull, the research would predict lower satisfaction.

## 3. Pay matters, but moderate pay increases are usually weaker predictors than work content

Pay is not irrelevant, but its relationship with overall job satisfaction is smaller than many people expect.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction: pay level correlates only modestly with overall job satisfaction, around `r ≈ .15`. It correlates more with satisfaction with pay specifically, but less with global job satisfaction.
- Income and subjective well-being research — e.g. **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**; **Killingsworth, 2021**; **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023** — suggests that more income is associated with higher well-being, but mostly logarithmically and with diminishing marginal effect. At already-high incomes, “a bit higher salary” usually has a smaller effect than differences in daily experience, autonomy, stress, meaning, and relationships.

So if the Google offer were dramatically financially life-changing — e.g. enabling financial independence much earlier — that would be different. But if it is only “a bit higher,” the research suggests pay alone should not dominate intrinsic job-quality factors.

## 4. Intrinsic motivation is especially important for creative/research work

For complex, creative, research-like work, intrinsic motivation seems particularly important.

- **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014**, meta-analysis: intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially quality of performance; extrinsic incentives predict quantity more than quality.
- **Amabile**’s research on creativity finds that intrinsic motivation supports creativity, while controlling extrinsic pressures can sometimes undermine it.
- **Deci, Koestner & Ryan, 1999**, meta-analysis: expected tangible rewards can undermine intrinsic motivation for already-interesting tasks, though the real-world workplace picture is more nuanced.

For a research engineer, this matters. If the Anthropic role is genuinely intellectually alive for you, that may not only make you happier — it may also make you better at the work, which compounds into reputation, learning, and future opportunities.

## 5. Meaningful progress is a major driver of day-to-day work happiness

A very relevant result comes from Teresa Amabile and Steven Kramer’s diary studies.

- **Amabile & Kramer, 2011**, *The Progress Principle*: in diary data from knowledge workers, the strongest driver of positive “inner work life” was making progress in meaningful work. Setbacks in meaningful work were among the strongest negative events.

This is important because “interesting” and “meaningful” are not identical. A job can be technically interesting but low-progress, or strategically meaningful but technically boring. The research suggests asking:

> In which role will I most often feel that I made real progress on something that matters?

## 6. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

Work is a large fraction of waking life, so job satisfaction does affect broader well-being.

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989**, meta-analysis: job and life satisfaction are substantially correlated.
- **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010**, meta-analysis: evidence suggests a reciprocal relationship — happier people are more satisfied at work, but job satisfaction also contributes to subjective well-being.

So “I can tolerate a boring job and be happy outside work” is sometimes true, but the base-rate evidence says work satisfaction tends to spill over into general life satisfaction.

## 7. Regret research: people often regret missed ideal-self opportunities

There is less direct evidence on “do people regret choosing interesting work over money?” But general regret research is relevant.

- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: in the short run, people often regret actions; in the long run, they more often regret inactions — things they did not try.
- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: career and education are among the most common domains of regret.
- **Davidai & Gilovich, 2018**: people’s most enduring regrets tend to concern failures to live up to their “ideal self,” not just failures of duty or obligation.

This cuts both ways in your situation:

- If your ideal-self narrative is “I want to work on the most important, intellectually alive AI research problems,” leaving that may be regretted.
- If your ideal-self narrative is “I want to become a leader and see whether I can build/manage a team,” not taking the Google role may be regretted.

So the regret literature does not say “always choose interesting work.” It says: **long-term regret is often about not pursuing identity-defining possibilities.**

## 8. Beware the “salary/status maximization” trap

One nice adjacent finding:

- **Iyengar, Wells & Schwartz, 2006** studied graduating job seekers. “Maximizers” obtained higher-paying jobs but were less satisfied, more stressed, and more regretful than “satisficers.”

This does not mean optimizing is bad, but it suggests that choosing based on externally measurable factors — salary, brand, title — can backfire if it ignores subjective fit.

## 9. Important caveat: interesting work can also burn people out

The research is not “interesting work always wins.”

Interesting or meaningful work can be dangerous when combined with:

- excessive hours,
- low control,
- unclear success criteria,
- moral pressure,
- poor management,
- high emotional load,
- lack of recovery.

Relevant literatures include the **Job Demands–Resources model** — e.g. **Bakker & Demerouti, 2007** — and research on **harmonious vs obsessive passion** — e.g. **Vallerand et al., 2003**. Harmonious passion predicts well-being; obsessive passion can predict conflict, overwork, and burnout.

So the right comparison is not simply:

> interesting Anthropic job vs boring Google job

but rather:

> Which role has better total work design: interest, autonomy, learning, meaning, social support, workload, leadership growth, and future optionality?

## Evidence-based way to frame your decision

Based on the research, I would evaluate each role on these dimensions:

1. **Intrinsic interest**: Will I find the core problems intellectually alive?
2. **Autonomy**: Will I have meaningful control over what I work on and how?
3. **Mastery/learning**: Will I keep getting better at things I care about?
4. **Task significance**: Will the work feel important?
5. **Progress loops**: Will I frequently see meaningful progress?
6. **Person–role fit**: Does the daily activity match my strengths and values?
7. **Social environment**: Will I like and respect the people I work with?
8. **Leadership identity**: Is managing a small team intrinsically appealing, or mainly attractive as status/career advancement?
9. **Workload/recovery**: Which role is more sustainable?
10. **Optionality**: Which choice preserves or expands future options?

The research-backed conclusion would be:

> If the Google role is only modestly better paid but substantially less interesting and less meaningful, the literature would predict a real risk of lower satisfaction. But if leading a team is itself a deep source of motivation, autonomy, growth, and identity for you, then the Google role may not actually be “less intrinsically rewarding” — it may just be rewarding along a different axis.

So the key empirical question for you is probably not “salary vs interesting work.” It is:

> Is “leading a small team” a genuinely intrinsic interest for me, or an external marker I have wanted because it represents advancement?

That distinction is highly aligned with what the research says predicts long-term satisfaction.

It reports an overall correlation of 0.15 between pay level and pay satisfaction, and 0.14 for job satisfaction. answer:
Yes—there’s a lot of relevant research, though not much that cleanly randomizes people into “more interesting Anthropic-like job” vs “higher-paid Google team-lead job.” The strongest evidence is indirect but fairly consistent.

## Short version

Across organizational psychology, labor economics, and well-being research:

1. **Intrinsic job qualities**—interesting tasks, autonomy, learning, skill use, meaning, challenge, person–job fit—are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.
2. **Pay matters**, especially when it affects security, fairness, lifestyle, or major life goals, but among already well-paid professionals, the marginal effect of “somewhat more salary” on happiness is usually much smaller than the effect of day-to-day job quality.
3. **Leadership could count as “interesting work”** if what you truly want is mentoring, strategy, leverage, and responsibility. But if the role mostly replaces intellectually exciting research with coordination, politics, and execution management, the literature would predict a real risk of lower satisfaction.
4. **Regret research** is less direct, but long-term regrets tend to be about missed opportunities, unrealized identities, and paths not taken more than about not maximizing income. That said, people can also regret staying in “meaningful/interesting” jobs that consume their life or under-reward them.

So the research-backed framing is less “interesting work vs salary” and more:

> Which role gives you more autonomy, competence/growth, relatedness, meaning, and sustainable daily experience?

Those factors are repeatedly associated with well-being.

---

## 1. Interesting work and job satisfaction: strong evidence

A large body of work comes from the **Job Characteristics Model** and later work-design research.

Classic and meta-analytic findings show that jobs are more satisfying and motivating when they have:

- **Autonomy**
- **Skill variety**
- **Task identity** — seeing a whole piece of work through
- **Task significance** — feeling the work matters
- **Feedback**
- **Challenge/complexity**
- **Opportunities to learn and use skills**

Relevant work:

- Hackman & Oldham, 1976/1980 — Job Characteristics Model.
- Fried & Ferris, 1987 — meta-analysis supporting relationships between enriched job characteristics and satisfaction/motivation.
- Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007 — large meta-analysis of work design. Motivational job characteristics such as autonomy, task significance, feedback, and complexity show medium-to-large relationships with job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, and lower turnover intentions.

This is probably the closest literature to “is interesting work important?” The answer is yes: **the structure and content of the work itself are major predictors of job satisfaction.**

A related literature on **person–job fit** finds even stronger associations. Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005, meta-analyzed fit research and found that person–job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction and organizational commitment and negatively related to intent to quit. In plain English: people are much happier when the work fits their abilities, interests, and values.

---

## 2. Meaningful work also matters a lot

“Interesting” is not exactly the same as “meaningful,” but they often overlap, especially in research roles.

Meta-analytic evidence suggests that **meaningful work** is strongly associated with:

- Job satisfaction
- Engagement
- Life satisfaction
- Lower withdrawal intentions
- General well-being

See for example:

- Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay, 2019, “Outcomes of meaningful work: A meta-analysis.”

The caveat: meaningful work is not a free lunch. Other research on “callings” and passion suggests that people in highly meaningful jobs can tolerate bad conditions for too long, overwork, or accept underpayment. Bunderson & Thompson’s work on zookeepers is a well-known example: people with a sense of calling accepted personal sacrifice because the work felt meaningful.

So the lesson is not “always choose the more meaningful job.” It is:

> Meaningful/interesting work is a major well-being factor, but only if the job is sustainable and not exploitative.

---

## 3. Intrinsic motivation often predicts performance and satisfaction better than external rewards

Self-Determination Theory is highly relevant here. It argues that people thrive when work satisfies three psychological needs:

- **Autonomy** — I have agency.
- **Competence** — I am growing and using my abilities.
- **Relatedness** — I am connected to people I respect/care about.

In work settings, satisfaction of these needs is associated with well-being, engagement, and performance. See Deci & Ryan’s work and workplace reviews/meta-analyses such as Van den Broeck et al., 2016.

Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014, meta-analyzed intrinsic motivation and extrinsic incentives. They found that intrinsic motivation and incentives both matter, but intrinsic motivation is especially important for complex, quality-oriented work. That is very relevant for research engineering, where the output is not just “more units” but judgment, creativity, taste, and persistence under uncertainty.

---

## 4. Pay: important, but usually weaker than people expect once you are comfortable

There are two related literatures: pay and job satisfaction, and income and subjective well-being.

### Pay and job satisfaction

Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010, meta-analyzed the relationship between pay and job satisfaction. A commonly cited result is that **absolute pay level has only a weak relationship with job satisfaction**, roughly around `r ≈ .15` depending on correction/model.

That does not mean pay is irrelevant. Pay affects:

- Security
- Perceived fairness
- Status
- Options
- Family/life planning
- Ability to leave bad jobs
- Ability to take future risks

But as a predictor of “do I like my job?”, pay is usually much weaker than work content, autonomy, manager quality, and fit.

### Income and happiness

Kahneman & Deaton, 2010, famously found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a threshold, while life evaluation kept rising. Later work by Killingsworth, 2021, and Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023, complicated this: well-being often continues rising with income, but roughly logarithmically, and with important heterogeneity.

The practical implication is:

> If the salary difference meaningfully changes your financial security or life options, it matters. If you are already highly compensated and the increase is only marginal, the expected happiness gain is likely modest relative to daily job quality.

For someone already in elite tech/AI compensation, “a bit higher salary” is unlikely to dominate job content unless the money has a specific, high-value use.

---

## 5. Scientists and researchers often trade pay for intellectual freedom

There is especially relevant research on scientists.

Stern, 2004, “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”, studied job choices among scientists and found evidence that scientists accept lower wages for jobs that allow more “science-like” activity, such as publishing, intellectual autonomy, and participation in the scientific community. The estimated wage tradeoff was substantial in his sample.

Sauermann & Roach, 2014, similarly studied PhD scientists’ preferences and found substantial heterogeneity: many scientists value publishing, freedom, and research orientation enough to sacrifice salary, though not all do.

This does not prove those people are happier ex post, but it shows that for research-oriented people, **intellectual freedom and interesting problems are often not small perks; they are core job attributes with large perceived value.**

For a Research Engineer, this seems directly relevant. If the Anthropic role gives you unusually high access to frontier problems, intellectual peers, and meaningful research direction, that is not just “fun.” It is a major non-wage component of the job.

---

## 6. Leadership: potentially very valuable, but depends on the actual work

You said the Google offer includes leading a small team, which is something you always wanted. Research would not treat that simply as an extrinsic perk. Leadership can increase several well-being-relevant factors:

- Autonomy
- Responsibility
- Task significance
- Social connection
- Mentoring/teaching
- Identity development
- Career capital
- Sense of impact

So if leading a team genuinely satisfies your needs for growth, impact, and relatedness, it could compensate for less interesting technical content.

But the research on job demands/resources suggests a warning. Leadership also often adds:

- Role conflict
- Meetings
- People problems
- Performance-management stress
- Less maker time
- Political constraints
- Responsibility without control

In the Job Demands–Resources model, resources like autonomy and support improve engagement, while demands like overload, ambiguity, and interpersonal conflict predict burnout.

So the key empirical question is not “team lead good or bad?” It is:

> Does this specific lead role increase autonomy, competence, meaning, and relatedness more than it reduces intellectual challenge and deep-work satisfaction?

A small-team technical lead role can be wonderful. A coordination-heavy execution-manager role can be much less satisfying for someone who loves research.

---

## 7. Regret research: indirect but relevant

There is not much direct research saying, “people who chose interesting work over salary later did/did not regret it.” But regret research offers some clues.

Gilovich & Medvec, 1995, found that in the short run people often regret actions, but over the long run people more often regret **inactions**—things they did not try, paths they did not pursue, opportunities they let pass.

Roese & Summerville, 2005, found that major life regrets often cluster around education, career, romance, and self-development. The general principle is that people regret domains where they see opportunity and personal growth as having been possible.

This cuts both ways in your case:

- You might regret leaving unusually interesting frontier work.
- You might regret not trying leadership when you had a concrete chance.
- You might regret optimizing for prestige/pay over daily intellectual fulfillment.
- You might regret staying too long in an IC/research role if your desired identity is “builder of teams.”

So the regret literature does not say “stay” or “go.” It says people often regret not pursuing personally meaningful possible selves.

---

## 8. What the literature would predict for your specific tradeoff

If I translate the evidence into predictions:

### Staying at Anthropic is more likely to be good if:

- The work is genuinely absorbing and meaningful most days.
- You have high autonomy.
- You are learning rapidly.
- You are surrounded by unusually strong peers.
- You feel the work fits your identity and long-term trajectory.
- The salary difference does not materially change your life.
- You can plausibly get leadership/mentorship opportunities later, either at Anthropic or elsewhere.

### Moving to Google is more likely to be good if:

- Leading people is itself deeply motivating to you, not just a title.
- The team’s mission is still sufficiently meaningful.
- You will have real autonomy, not just responsibility.
- You will learn management, strategy, and organizational leverage.
- The compensation meaningfully improves your security/options.
- The less-interesting technical work is offset by more interesting human/organizational work.

### Moving is riskier if:

- The main attractor is salary/status.
- “Lead a small team” actually means project management without much agency.
- The work is much less aligned with your intellectual taste.
- You would lose access to rare frontier problems and peers.
- You expect to miss deep technical/research work.

---

## My research-based takeaway

The evidence does **not** support treating “interesting work” as a luxury good. It is one of the central predictors of job satisfaction, especially for complex creative work.

But “leading a team” may itself be a form of interesting work if it gives you autonomy, growth, mentorship, and impact.

So the crux is probably:

> Is the Google role less interesting only technically, or less interesting overall?

If it is less interesting technically but more interesting as a leadership/growth opportunity, the tradeoff is real. If it is less interesting overall and only better paid/more titled, the research would lean toward staying with the more intrinsically motivating role, especially if you are already financially comfortable.

answer:
Short answer: yes—there is fairly strong evidence that “the work itself” matters a lot. In organizational psychology, intrinsic job features—interesting/challenging work, autonomy, skill use, meaning, learning, feedback, good colleagues—are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement. Absolute pay matters, but its marginal association with happiness/job satisfaction is usually much weaker, especially once you are already highly paid. The evidence on “regret” is much thinner, but affective-forecasting research suggests people often overweight salient, comparable things like salary/title and underweight day-to-day experience.

Below are the most relevant research areas.

---

## 1. “Interesting work” is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model**: Hackman & Oldham argued that jobs are motivating when they provide:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

These are close to what people usually mean by “interesting work.”

Meta-analyses generally support this. For example, **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007** reviewed work-design studies and found that motivational job features—autonomy, task significance, task variety, feedback, job complexity—are substantially related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, performance, and lower strain. Correlations with job satisfaction are often in the roughly **0.3–0.5** range, which is large by workplace-psychology standards.

Related evidence comes from **person–job fit** research. **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005** meta-analyzed fit at work and found that person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction and negatively associated with intent to quit. In plain terms: people are happier when the job uses the abilities and interests they actually want to use.

**Implication for you:** if “less interesting” means meaningfully less autonomy, less intellectual challenge, less learning, or less mission relevance, the literature would treat that as a major cost—not a small lifestyle preference.

---

## 2. Intrinsic motivation and psychological needs matter more than many extrinsic markers

Self-Determination Theory, associated with **Deci & Ryan**, argues that well-being and motivation depend heavily on satisfaction of three needs:

- autonomy  
- competence  
- relatedness  

Workplace reviews and meta-analyses, e.g. **Gagné & Deci 2005** and **Van den Broeck et al. 2016**, find that need satisfaction at work is strongly related to job satisfaction, engagement, vitality, and lower burnout.

This is especially relevant for research engineering. A role that gives you autonomy, technical mastery, intellectual ownership, and trusted collaborators is hitting the core predictors of motivation. A role with more money and title but less autonomy/competence/meaning may not compensate psychologically.

That said, leading a team can itself satisfy autonomy, competence, and relatedness—if the leadership work is real and you enjoy it. So the right comparison is not “research vs management title,” but:

> Which role gives me more of the experiences I actually find intrinsically rewarding?

---

## 3. Pay matters, but marginal pay is usually a weaker predictor than people expect

Pay is not irrelevant. It affects security, options, fairness, status, family constraints, and long-term freedom. But the association between **absolute pay level** and job satisfaction is surprisingly modest.

A useful meta-analysis is **Judge et al. 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction.” They found that pay level has only a small relationship with job satisfaction—around **r ≈ .15**—and a somewhat stronger but still moderate relationship with pay satisfaction. In other words, richer workers are not dramatically more satisfied with their jobs just because they are paid more.

On general well-being, the income literature is nuanced:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a threshold, while life evaluation continued rising.
- **Killingsworth 2021** found experienced well-being continues to rise with income, even above previous thresholds.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** reconciled the findings: for many people well-being keeps rising with log income, but the effect is not huge, and for the least happy group it may plateau.

The practical takeaway is: a salary increase can matter, especially if it changes your financial security or life options. But if both jobs already pay very well, the marginal happiness effect of extra salary is usually smaller than the effect of day-to-day job quality.

**Implication for you:** if Google’s salary increase meaningfully changes your financial independence timeline, family security, immigration risk, etc., give it real weight. If it is “nice but not life-changing,” research suggests not letting it dominate the decision.

---

## 4. Meaningful work is strongly associated with satisfaction and well-being

There is also a literature on **meaningful work** and “calling.” For example:

- **Wrzesniewski et al. 1997** distinguished people who view work as a job, career, or calling; those with a calling generally report higher work and life satisfaction.
- **Allan et al. 2019** meta-analyzed meaningful-work studies and found meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, commitment, and general well-being.

The caveat: meaningful work can also make people vulnerable to overwork or exploitation. **Bunderson & Thompson 2009**, studying zookeepers, found that a sense of calling produced deep meaning but also willingness to accept low pay and sacrifice. So meaning is powerful, but not always benign.

**Implication:** if Anthropic’s work feels unusually meaningful and aligned with your values, that is not a fuzzy intangible; it is one of the best-supported predictors of work satisfaction. But you should still check whether the role is sustainable.

---

## 5. Promotion/management often helps, but not always

You mentioned that leading a small team is something you have always wanted. That is important. Research on promotions and status suggests mixed effects:

- Promotions can increase job satisfaction, partly via pay, autonomy, and recognition.
- But they can also increase stress, hours, responsibility, and political/coordination work.
- Some studies, e.g. **Boyce & Oswald 2012**, suggest promotions do not reliably improve health or overall well-being and may increase mental strain.

Management is not a single thing. A “small team lead” role could mean:

1. real technical leadership and mentoring, which you may find deeply satisfying; or  
2. project management, meetings, performance reviews, and reduced technical depth, which may be less satisfying if your core motivation is research/engineering.

So the relevant research-backed question is:

> Does this leadership role increase autonomy, mastery, relatedness, and meaning—or mostly status, meetings, and responsibility?

If the former, the Google offer may be psychologically attractive despite a less interesting technical area. If the latter, the title may not compensate.

---

## 6. People often mispredict what will make them happy

There is relevant decision-making research, though not specific to Anthropic-vs-Google-type choices.

Affective-forecasting work by **Gilbert, Wilson, Kahneman, Hsee** and others shows that people often overweight salient, easily compared features when choosing, and underweight daily lived experience. Examples:

- **Hsee & Hastie 2006** discuss how people’s choices often diverge from what later produces experienced utility.
- **Hsee’s “distinction bias”** research suggests that when comparing options side-by-side, people overweight attributes that are easy to compare—salary, title, brand—and underweight attributes that matter in isolation.
- **Schkade & Kahneman 1998** showed a related “focusing illusion”: when people focus on one factor, they exaggerate its importance to overall happiness.

There is also the **commuting paradox** literature. **Stutzer & Frey 2008** found that people with longer commutes report lower life satisfaction, and the higher pay or housing benefits they receive often do not fully compensate. This is a nice example of people underweighting repeated daily experience relative to objective benefits.

**Implication:** when choosing between jobs, people may overvalue the visible deltas—salary, brand, title—and undervalue the recurring experience of “what am I actually doing at 10:30am on a Tuesday?”

---

## 7. What about regret specifically?

Direct evidence on “Do people regret choosing interesting work over higher pay?” is limited. Regret research is broader.

A classic review/meta-analysis by **Roese & Summerville 2005** found that people’s biggest regrets often involve education, career, romance, and parenting. Career is a major regret domain, but the research usually does not decompose it cleanly into “chose boring high-paying job” vs “chose interesting lower-paying job.”

Another robust finding from **Gilovich & Medvec 1995** is that in the short run people regret actions, while in the long run they often regret inactions—missed opportunities. That cuts both ways for you:

- You might regret leaving uniquely interesting work.
- Or you might regret not trying leadership when you had the chance.

So the regret literature does not give a simple answer. It says to pay attention to personally meaningful opportunities you may later view as “the path not taken.”

---

## A research-informed way to frame your decision

The evidence suggests you should not reduce the choice to “salary vs interesting work.” Instead, score each role on the factors most associated with durable job satisfaction:

1. **Autonomy** — Will I control important decisions?  
2. **Competence/mastery** — Will I keep learning and doing work I’m unusually good at?  
3. **Meaning/task significance** — Does the work matter to me?  
4. **Interest/flow** — Will the day-to-day problems absorb me?  
5. **Social environment** — Manager, peers, team quality, trust.  
6. **Workload/sustainability** — Hours, stress, burnout risk.  
7. **Growth path** — Does this open future roles I genuinely want?  
8. **Compensation/security** — Is the salary difference life-changing or mostly marginal?  
9. **Leadership reality** — Is the Google role true leadership, or mostly coordination?

Based on the literature, the strongest general prediction would be:

> If Anthropic is substantially more interesting/meaningful and both jobs already pay enough, staying is more likely to preserve day-to-day happiness.  
> But if leading a team is itself a deep intrinsic goal for you—not just status—and the Google role gives real autonomy, mentorship, and growth, then the leadership opportunity could be a legitimate psychological benefit that compensates for less interesting technical content.

The key distinction is whether Google is offering **more of what research says matters**—autonomy, mastery, relatedness, meaning—or mainly more of what people often overestimate: salary, title, and prestige.

Now, I’m pondering whether people in more interesting jobs tend to be happier. Generally, if "interesting" means having intrinsic motivation, autonomy, meaningfulness, and skill use, then yes, it’s often linked to greater job satisfaction. **Considering career success factors**

Objective measures align with tangible accomplishments. However, subjective career success leans more towards satisfaction, which is shaped by the meaningfulness of work.

answer:
Short version: yes, there is quite a lot of evidence that “interesting work” — usually operationalized as autonomy, skill use, task variety, meaningfulness, person–job fit, intrinsic motivation, and growth — is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, lower burnout, and sometimes life satisfaction. Objective pay matters, but above financial security its marginal association with day-to-day well-being and job satisfaction is usually smaller than people expect. Leadership/promotion is mixed: it can increase autonomy, status, learning, and meaning, but also stress, meetings, politics, and role conflict.

There is much less direct research on “do people later regret choosing the interesting job over the higher-paid/managerial one?” Regret research says career is one of the most common regret domains, and long-run regrets often involve missed opportunities — but it does not cleanly answer this exact tradeoff.

## 1. Interesting/meaningful/autonomous work is a major predictor of job satisfaction

The classic framework is Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model. Jobs tend to be more motivating and satisfying when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity — seeing a whole piece of work through  
- task significance — the work matters  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

A large meta-analysis by Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007 found that these “motivational job characteristics” have substantial associations with job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, performance, and lower withdrawal. Autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, and social support are repeatedly among the strongest work-design predictors of satisfaction and engagement.

Relatedly, person–job fit is a strong predictor. Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005 meta-analyzed fit research and found that person–job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intention to quit. In plain English: people are happier when the work uses their abilities, matches their interests, and supplies what they value.

Self-determination theory research says something similar: people do better when work satisfies autonomy, competence, and relatedness. Meta-analytic work by Van den Broeck et al., 2016 finds that these needs at work are strongly associated with engagement, well-being, job satisfaction, and lower burnout.

**Implication:** if “interesting” means “I get to use my best skills on problems I care about, with autonomy and learning,” that is not a small factor. It is one of the central factors in the job-satisfaction literature.

## 2. Meaningful work is especially predictive

Meaningful work/calling research also points in the same direction. Wrzesniewski et al., 1997 found that people who experienced their work as a “calling” reported higher job and life satisfaction, independent of occupation. Later meta-analyses, e.g. Allan et al., 2019, find meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, organizational commitment, life satisfaction, and lower depression/withdrawal.

Caveat: some of this is correlational. Happier people may perceive their work as more meaningful. But the association is large and consistent, and longitudinal/job-redesign evidence supports at least some causal effect.

**Implication:** if your current work feels unusually meaningful or intellectually alive, the research would suggest taking that seriously, not treating it as a “soft” factor.

## 3. Pay matters, but objective pay has a surprisingly weak relationship with job satisfaction

A useful citation here is Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010, a meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction. They found that actual pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction — roughly much smaller than fit, autonomy, work design, or meaning.

For broader happiness, the income literature is nuanced:

- Kahneman & Deaton, 2010: in U.S. data, emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, then flattened; life evaluation kept rising.  
- Killingsworth, 2021: using experience sampling, well-being continued to rise with log income, with less evidence of a sharp plateau.  
- Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023: reconciled these results — for many people happiness keeps rising with income, but among the least happy there is more of a plateau; effects are real but not huge.

The key is that income effects are usually approximately logarithmic: going from $50k to $100k matters much more than going from $300k to $350k, at least for personal well-being. At high compensation levels, extra salary can still matter for savings, family, donations, optionality, or status, but the marginal effect on daily happiness is often modest.

**Implication:** if the Google offer is “a bit higher salary” and both jobs already meet your financial needs, the happiness literature would usually not treat salary as the dominant factor. If the salary difference changes your financial security, visa situation, family options, ability to buy a house, or ability to take future risks, then it matters more.

## 4. Promotion/management is mixed: more autonomy and growth, but also more stress

The evidence on promotions and management is not as clean as the evidence on job design. Promotions often increase job satisfaction, partly through higher pay, status, and perceived progress. For example, Kosteas, 2011 found positive effects of promotion on job satisfaction.

But promotions can also increase stress and reduce well-being if they add demands without matching autonomy/resources. Some panel studies find that promotion is associated with increased mental strain, even when satisfaction/status rises. This fits the broader job demands–resources model: high responsibility can be energizing when paired with autonomy, support, and competence; it becomes draining when paired with politics, overload, ambiguity, or lack of control.

For your specific case, “leading a small team” is not just an extrinsic factor. It may satisfy intrinsic needs too: autonomy, mentoring, influence, competence, social relatedness, and identity growth. But it may also mean less time doing the research/engineering work you currently find fascinating.

**Implication:** the key research-backed question is not “manager vs IC,” but: what will your actual week look like? If leadership means shaping a technical agenda with people you like, it may be highly satisfying. If it means meetings, performance management, bureaucracy, and less intellectual engagement, the title may not compensate.

## 5. Regret research: career is a common regret domain, but the exact tradeoff is under-studied

Roese & Summerville, 2005 reviewed regret research and found that education, career, romance, and parenting are among the most common life-regret domains. Another robust finding from Gilovich & Medvec, 1995 is that short-term regrets are often about actions, while long-term regrets are often about inactions — missed chances, unexplored paths, things one never tried.

This could cut both ways:

- You might regret leaving unusually interesting work for a less interesting but higher-status/salary path.  
- You might regret not trying leadership if it is something you have “always wanted to do.”  

The regret literature does not say “always choose interesting work” or “always take the promotion.” It says people often regret salient unrealized possibilities, especially when they feel they never tested them.

**Research-backed practical implication:** if possible, reduce irreversibility. Try to get management experience where you are, negotiate a tech-lead/people-lead hybrid, ask Google for clarity on returning to IC/research, or talk to people who held that exact role and left. Regret tends to be lower when people feel they made an informed, self-concordant choice rather than being pulled by prestige or fear.

## 6. Affective forecasting: people often overweight salient, measurable factors

Affective forecasting research by Wilson & Gilbert and others finds that people often mispredict how much future events will affect their happiness. We overweight focal, easily compared attributes — salary, title, company brand — and underweight repeated daily experience — meetings, commute, manager quality, autonomy, colleagues, boredom, stress.

This is related to Kahneman’s “focusing illusion”: nothing is as important as it seems while you are thinking about it.

**Implication:** compare the expected “typical Tuesday” in each job, not just the offer letter. Daily work content is likely to matter more than the abstract prestige/title delta.

## My reading of the literature for your tradeoff

Research would generally say:

1. **Do not undervalue interesting, meaningful, high-fit work.** It is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.  
2. **A modest salary increase, once already highly paid, is unlikely by itself to dominate well-being.** It may still matter for strategic/life reasons.  
3. **Leadership could be intrinsically valuable if you genuinely want that kind of work.** The fact that it is “something I always wanted to do” is important; it is not merely extrinsic.  
4. **Management is not automatically happier or better.** Its effect depends heavily on autonomy, support, workload, role clarity, and whether you enjoy people-development/political/coordination work.  
5. **Regret evidence suggests testing desired paths when possible**, especially if the opportunity is hard to recreate, but it does not imply sacrificing core work fit for title/salary.

A concise research-backed decision question would be:

> Which option gives you more expected autonomy, competence/growth, relatedness, meaning, and day-to-day interest over the next 2–3 years — after adjusting for financial security and reversibility?

If the Google role is less interesting only in research topic but gives you real autonomy, mentoring, leadership growth, and future optionality, the literature would see real upside. If it is less interesting in the sense of worse person–job fit and more bureaucracy, then the salary/title premium may not compensate much for happiness.

answer:
Yes—there is a lot of relevant research, though not much that directly asks “do people regret choosing a more interesting job over a higher-paid one?” The strongest evidence is about job satisfaction, well-being, turnover, and work motivation.

## Short version

The research generally says:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction and engagement.**
2. **Pay matters, but after financial security/fairness, pay level is usually a weaker predictor of job satisfaction than work content, autonomy, fit, and relationships.**
3. **People often adapt to salary/title bumps; they do not as easily adapt to spending most of their week doing work they find boring, pointless, or misaligned.**
4. **But “leading a small team” may itself be intrinsically valuable**—autonomy, influence, mentoring, mastery—so this is not simply “interesting work vs money.” It may be “technical/research interest vs leadership-growth interest.”

The evidence would usually make me treat “much less interesting work” as a serious cost, not a minor preference.

---

## 1. Job design research: autonomy, variety, meaning, feedback matter a lot

A major line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman & Oldham. It studies features like:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- feedback,
- meaningfulness of the work.

Meta-analyses generally find that these job characteristics have substantial associations with job satisfaction, internal motivation, and lower turnover intentions.

Relevant studies:

- **Loher et al. 1985**, meta-analysis of job characteristics and job satisfaction.
- **Fried & Ferris 1987**, meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, large meta-analysis of work design.

Typical finding: autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, and social support often correlate with job satisfaction in the roughly **r = .30 to .50** range, depending on the measure. In organizational psychology, that is a meaningful effect.

So when people say “I like the work itself,” the research treats that as central, not cosmetic.

For your case, “interesting work” likely maps to several high-value dimensions:

- intellectual challenge,
- skill use,
- learning,
- novelty,
- perceived importance,
- autonomy,
- identity/fit.

Those are all empirically associated with satisfaction and engagement.

---

## 2. Person–job fit is very predictive

Another relevant literature is **person–environment fit**, especially **person–job fit**: do your abilities, interests, and values match the role?

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005**, *Personnel Psychology*.

They found that **person–job fit** was strongly associated with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit. Approximate corrected correlations often cited:

- person–job fit with job satisfaction: around **r = .56**,
- with organizational commitment: around **r = .47**,
- with intention to quit: around **r = –.46**.

That is quite large by social-science standards.

There is also vocational-interest research based on Holland/RIASEC-type fit. The effects are usually smaller than broad person–job fit, but still reliable. Meta-analyses such as **Assouline & Meir 1987** and **Tsabari, Tziner & Meir 2005** find that interest congruence predicts job satisfaction, often around **r ≈ .20**, with variation by measure and population.

Interpretation: people whose work matches their interests and identity tend to be more satisfied and less likely to want to leave. The effect is not deterministic, but it is robust.

---

## 3. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being

There is also a literature on **meaningful work**.

A useful review/meta-analysis:

- **Allan et al. 2019**, “Outcomes of Meaningful Work.”

They find that meaningful work is positively associated with:

- job satisfaction,
- work engagement,
- life satisfaction,
- organizational commitment,

and negatively associated with:

- withdrawal intentions,
- burnout-related outcomes,
- depression/negative affect.

This matters because “interesting work” at a place whose mission you care about may not merely be fun; it may provide meaning, identity, and motivation.

That said, meaningful work can have a downside: people may tolerate worse boundaries or overwork because the work feels important. So meaningful/interesting work is good, but not if it comes with chronic burnout, low control, or unsustainable demands.

---

## 4. Pay matters, but its relationship with job satisfaction is weaker than many expect

The most directly relevant pay result is:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.

They found that **pay level** is only weakly to modestly related to overall job satisfaction. A commonly cited estimate is around:

- pay level with job satisfaction: roughly **r = .15**,
- pay level with pay satisfaction: somewhat higher, but still not huge.

So salary matters, but pay is usually less predictive of overall job satisfaction than job content, autonomy, fit, and relationships.

For subjective well-being more generally, the income literature says:

- Income improves life evaluation and reduces stress, especially at lower levels.
- The relationship is approximately logarithmic: each additional dollar matters less as income rises.
- The old **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** result suggested emotional well-being plateaued around $75k/year in 2010 dollars.
- **Killingsworth 2021** found experienced well-being continues rising above that.
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers 2023** reconciled the results: for many people, well-being keeps rising with income, but for the least happy group it may plateau around a fairly high income.

The important practical point: **higher salary is not irrelevant**, but at already-high compensation levels, a further increase often buys less day-to-day happiness than people predict—unless it materially changes your security, family options, ability to buy time, early retirement, etc.

Also, pay fairness matters. Being underpaid relative to peers can be demotivating even if absolute income is high.

---

## 5. People value non-wage job attributes enough to trade salary for them

Labor-economics studies using stated-preference or field-experimental methods find that workers are often willing to give up nontrivial salary for better working conditions.

Examples:

- **Mas & Pallais 2017**, on valuing alternative work arrangements. Workers show willingness to trade pay for flexibility, work-from-home, predictable scheduling, etc., though with large individual heterogeneity.
- **Maestas et al.**, work on the value of working conditions in the US, estimates that non-wage amenities—autonomy, meaningfulness, flexibility, physical demands, scheduling, job security—can be worth substantial fractions of wages to workers.

These studies are not exactly about “interesting AI research versus less interesting management,” but they reinforce the point that job quality is economically valuable. People do not behave as if salary is the only thing that matters.

---

## 6. Job changes often have a “honeymoon–hangover” pattern

Research on job changes often finds that satisfaction rises around a move, then partially fades.

A relevant paper:

- **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy 2005**, “The relationship between employee job change and job satisfaction: The honeymoon-hangover effect.”

People often experience a satisfaction bump after changing jobs because of novelty, optimism, higher pay, or escaping prior annoyances. But the bump can decline as the new job’s everyday realities become salient.

Implication: a new title, company, salary, or leadership opportunity may feel very attractive ex ante. Some of that may persist if the underlying job design is genuinely better. But some may be transient.

The enduring question is: **what will your Tuesday afternoon look like six months in?**

---

## 7. Direct regret evidence is weaker

You asked specifically whether people regret staying in more interesting jobs or choosing them over other factors. The direct evidence is limited.

The literature more commonly measures:

- job satisfaction,
- life satisfaction,
- turnover intentions,
- actual turnover,
- engagement,
- career satisfaction.

There is regret research showing that career is one of the major domains of life regret—e.g. **Roese & Summerville 2005**—and that long-run regrets often involve missed opportunities or inactions—e.g. **Gilovich & Medvec 1995**. But those studies do not cleanly answer “do people regret choosing interest over pay?”

So the strongest evidence is indirect: people with better person–job fit, meaningful work, autonomy, and interesting job content tend to be more satisfied, more engaged, and less likely to want to quit.

---

## How I would translate the research to your decision

The research does **not** imply “always choose the most intellectually interesting job.” It implies something more specific:

Choose the role with the better expected long-run fit on the dimensions that reliably predict work well-being:

1. **Autonomy**  
   Will you have control over technical direction, priorities, and how work is done?

2. **Competence/mastery**  
   Will you keep learning and using skills you care about?

3. **Meaning/task significance**  
   Does the work feel important to you?

4. **Interest/flow**  
   Will the actual day-to-day tasks be engaging?

5. **Relatedness/team quality**  
   Will you like and respect the people you work with?

6. **Manager/org support**  
   Will your manager and org make it easier or harder to do good work?

7. **Workload/burnout risk**  
   Is the role sustainable?

8. **Compensation/security**  
   Does the salary difference materially change your life, optionality, or stress?

9. **Growth path**  
   Does the role move you toward the kind of person/professional you want to become?

The Google offer may score lower on research/technical interest, but higher on leadership, autonomy, scope, and growth. If leading a small team is something you genuinely want, that is not merely an extrinsic reward. It may satisfy autonomy, competence, influence, mentoring, and identity needs.

But if the honest forecast is:

> “I will mostly spend my week on less interesting problems, with more coordination, less intellectual excitement, and the main benefits are salary/title/company brand,”

then the research would be skeptical that the higher salary alone will compensate, especially if your current salary is already comfortably high.

A useful heuristic from the evidence:

> If the salary bump does not materially change your life, and the new role is substantially worse on day-to-day interest, autonomy, meaning, or fit, the higher salary is unlikely to fully offset that in long-run satisfaction.  
> But if the leadership component is itself deeply motivating and gives you more autonomy/growth, the tradeoff may be much closer.

The key empirical distinction is not “interesting work vs money.” It is:

> Which role gives you more of the psychological ingredients that repeatedly predict durable work satisfaction: autonomy, mastery, meaning, fit, and good relationships?

I'm working as a Research Engineer in Anthropic doing very interesting stuff. I got a proposal from Google - a bit higher salary, leading a small team which is something I always wanted to do, but much less interesting. I wonder what to do. 

There must be some research on how important is doing interesting work comparing to other factors. Do people who stay in a more interesting job tend to be happier? Or do they regret that? Can you recall any results on that? I'm mostly interested in research results, not personal opinions.
