answer:
Short answer from the literature: **yes, “interesting work” / intrinsic motivation / fit / autonomy are consistently strong predictors of job satisfaction and often of broader well-being.** A modest salary increase, especially at already-high income levels, tends to have a much smaller association with day-to-day happiness. But “leading a small team” is not merely extrinsic: it may increase autonomy, impact, learning, status, and identity fit, which are also important.

Below are the research results I’d put most weight on.

---

## 1. Work content, autonomy, and challenge are among the strongest job-satisfaction predictors

A large work-design meta-analysis by **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007** looked at job characteristics such as autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback, problem-solving, and job complexity. These “motivational” characteristics are reliably associated with job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, engagement, and lower withdrawal.

Roughly, many of these correlations with job satisfaction are in the **r ≈ .30–.50** range, which is fairly large for field psychology. Autonomy, task significance, skill variety, feedback from the job, and job complexity all matter.

This is basically the empirical descendant of the **Hackman & Oldham Job Characteristics Model**: people tend to like jobs more when they experience:

- autonomy/control,
- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance/meaning,
- feedback,
- challenging but manageable demands.

“Interesting work” is not always measured directly, but it overlaps strongly with skill variety, problem-solving, complexity, learning, and intrinsic motivation.

---

## 2. Person–job fit is very strongly related to satisfaction

A major meta-analysis by **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005** found that **person–job fit** is highly related to job satisfaction, with correlations around **r ≈ .50+**. It also predicts organizational commitment and lower turnover intentions.

Vocational-interest congruence research — Holland-type “do my interests match my work?” studies — generally finds smaller but still meaningful associations with satisfaction and persistence, often around **r ≈ .20–.30**, depending on measurement.

So if “Anthropic work is deeply interesting to me” means “this role fits my core interests and identity,” the evidence says that is a major variable, not a luxury variable.

---

## 3. Job satisfaction is substantially related to life satisfaction

A meta-analysis by **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang, 2010** found job satisfaction correlates with subjective well-being/life satisfaction in the rough range of **r ≈ .30–.50**. The causality is partly bidirectional: happier people like their jobs more, but better jobs also appear to contribute to broader well-being.

So this is not just “people enjoy work while at work.” Your work experience spills into general life satisfaction.

---

## 4. Pay matters, but objective salary is usually a weaker predictor than work quality

A meta-analysis by **Judge et al., 2010** on pay and job satisfaction found that actual pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction, roughly **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction matters more than objective pay, but still usually less than fit, autonomy, social support, and intrinsic job features.

The broader income–well-being literature is nuanced:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**: emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** and **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023**: experienced well-being often continues to rise with log income, though not equally for everyone; for the least happy people, income may plateau sooner.

But the important practical point: at high income levels, a “bit higher salary” usually has a relatively small marginal effect on daily happiness unless it changes something concrete: financial security, family options, location, autonomy, ability to take risks, etc.

Pay also matters a lot if it feels unfair. Perceived inequity can poison satisfaction. But if both jobs are already very well-compensated, salary differences tend to be less predictive than day-to-day work experience.

---

## 5. Intrinsic vs extrinsic goals: people often overestimate money/status and underestimate daily experience

Research in self-determination theory — **Deci & Ryan** and many follow-ups — distinguishes intrinsic goals/needs from extrinsic rewards. Autonomy, competence, relatedness, and meaningful activity are robustly associated with well-being. Extrinsic rewards are not bad, but when they become the main reason for doing something, they often predict less well-being.

Relevant studies:

- **Kasser & Ryan**: stronger extrinsic aspirations — money, image, status — are generally associated with lower well-being, while intrinsic aspirations — growth, relationships, contribution — are associated with higher well-being.
- **Nickerson, Schwarz, Diener & Kahneman, 2003**: people who placed unusually high importance on financial success tended to earn more later, but this did not straightforwardly translate into higher life satisfaction; high financial aspirations can create a bigger aspiration gap.

This does not mean “never optimize for money/status.” It means: if the Google option is attractive mainly because of salary/title, the literature would be skeptical that those alone will compensate for less engaging work. If it is attractive because leading a team would satisfy autonomy, mastery, identity, and growth needs, that is a different story.

---

## 6. Leadership/management is mixed: more control and impact, but also more stress

Becoming a manager/team lead can improve some important job characteristics:

- more autonomy,
- more influence,
- more status,
- more task significance,
- more career capital,
- more social/mentoring satisfaction.

But it can also mean:

- less hands-on technical work,
- more meetings,
- more interpersonal conflict,
- more responsibility without full control,
- more stress.

Evidence is mixed. For example:

- **Johnston & Lee, 2013**, using longitudinal data, found promotions were associated with higher job satisfaction but also higher mental strain.
- **Sherman et al., 2012** found leaders sometimes show lower stress than non-leaders, apparently because perceived control buffers stress.

So “leading a small team” is not automatically good or bad. The key mediators are control, support, demands, and whether management itself is interesting to you.

---

## 7. Regret evidence is weaker, but career regrets are common

There is less direct research of the form: “people who chose interesting job over higher-paid less-interesting job later regretted it less.” That clean comparison is hard to study.

But some relevant regret findings:

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005** found that career is one of the most common domains of major life regret.
- People often regret domains where they had many opportunities and a strong sense of personal choice.
- Long-term regrets often involve perceived missed opportunities for growth, identity, relationships, or meaningful achievement — not just money.

There is also “commuting paradox” research, e.g. **Stutzer & Frey, 2008**, showing that people often accept worse daily conditions, such as long commutes, for compensation that does not fully offset the well-being cost. This is part of a broader affective-forecasting pattern: people overweight salient, legible changes — salary, title, prestige — and underweight repeated daily experience.

For your question, the analogy is: **if “less interesting” means every day is less absorbing, less meaningful, or less growth-producing, that cost is likely to be real and persistent.**

---

## Bottom line from research

If I compress the evidence:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous, well-matched work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **A modest salary increase is usually a weaker predictor, especially at high income levels.**
3. **Management/leadership can be very satisfying if it increases autonomy, impact, learning, and identity fit; it can be bad if it mostly adds bureaucracy and stress.**
4. **Direct regret evidence is limited, but people often mispredict by overweighting pay/status and underweighting daily work experience.**
5. The most research-grounded comparison is not “Anthropic vs Google,” but:  
   **Which role gives you more autonomy, competence growth, meaningful impact, social support, manageable demands, and fit with your interests?**

So the research does not simply say “stay with the more interesting job.” It says: **do not treat interesting work as a soft factor. It is empirically one of the central factors.** But if leading a team is itself a deeply interesting growth path for you, then the Google role may have more intrinsic value than “less interesting work” initially suggests.

answer:
Short version: the research base is pretty consistent that **intrinsic job qualities**—interesting/challenging work, autonomy, meaning, skill use, fit with one’s values/interests, good colleagues/manager—are usually much stronger predictors of job satisfaction than **moderate differences in pay**, especially once you are already well-paid. But “leading a small team” is not just an extrinsic factor: it can increase autonomy, impact, competence, mentoring/relatedness, and career fit. So the evidence does not say “always choose the more interesting technical work”; it says to compare the two jobs on the underlying drivers, not just “interesting vs salary.”

Some relevant findings:

| Factor | What research tends to find | Relevance to your case |
|---|---|---|
| Interesting/challenging work, autonomy, skill variety | Strongly related to job satisfaction, motivation, engagement | If Anthropic work is unusually intellectually alive, that is a big deal |
| Person–job/vocational fit | One of the strongest predictors of satisfaction and retention | If frontier AI research is unusually aligned with your identity/interests, leaving may hurt |
| Pay | Positively related to satisfaction, but effect is comparatively weak after comfortable income levels | A “bit higher salary” may not move happiness much unless it materially changes security/freedom |
| Meaning/mission | Strongly related to engagement, satisfaction, lower turnover intentions | Depends how meaningful you find each company/team’s work |
| Management/leadership opportunity | Can increase satisfaction via autonomy, status, growth, influence; can also add stress/admin | The Google role might be better if “leading a team” is itself intrinsically motivating to you |
| Regret | Career is a common regret domain; long-term regrets often involve foregone opportunities, but evidence does not resolve “stay vs leave” | You may regret missing leadership, or regret leaving rare interesting work; make it reversible if possible |

### 1. Job design research: interesting/autonomous work matters a lot

The classic line of research is the **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman & Oldham. It argues that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  

Meta-analyses generally support this. For example:

- **Fried & Ferris, 1987**, meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model: core job characteristics were reliably related to satisfaction, internal motivation, and performance.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, large meta-analysis of work design: motivational features such as autonomy, task significance, feedback, problem-solving demands, and job complexity had substantial relationships with job satisfaction, motivation, performance, and lower turnover.

“Interesting work” is not always measured directly, but it usually maps onto **complexity, problem solving, skill variety, autonomy, learning, and meaningfulness**. Those are consistently important.

For a research engineer, this is especially relevant: creative/technical work often depends heavily on autonomy, challenge, and intrinsic motivation. Research on creativity also finds that intrinsic motivation is important for creative output; see, e.g., **Amabile’s work on creativity** and **Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014**, a meta-analysis showing intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially quality of performance.

### 2. Person–job fit is a major predictor of satisfaction

A very relevant literature is **person–environment fit** or **person–job fit**.

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, meta-analysis: person–job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit.
- Vocational interest congruence studies, e.g. **Assouline & Meir, 1987** and later reviews, find that when people’s interests match their work environment, they tend to be more satisfied and persistent.

So if “interesting AI research” is not merely pleasant but central to your sense of fit—“this is the kind of problem I want my working life to be about”—that should get a lot of weight.

### 3. Pay matters, but less than people often expect at high income levels

The pay literature is nuanced.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction: pay level is positively related to job satisfaction, but the correlation is fairly modest. Pay satisfaction and perceived fairness matter more than raw pay.
- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**: income strongly predicts life evaluation, but day-to-day emotional well-being showed diminishing returns and, in their dataset, plateaued around $75k/year in 2008 dollars.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** challenged the plateau result, finding experienced well-being continues rising with log income even above that level.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** reconciled some of this: for many people well-being continues to rise with income, but the effect is still approximately logarithmic and heterogeneous.

The practical implication: a large increase from financial insecurity to comfort can matter enormously. But for someone already in a high-paying technical role, a “bit higher salary” probably has a smaller expected happiness effect than changes in autonomy, work content, manager/team quality, mission fit, and time allocation.

### 4. Meaning and autonomy are not fluffy variables; they are robust predictors

Self-determination theory argues that well-being and motivation depend heavily on satisfaction of three needs:

- autonomy  
- competence  
- relatedness  

Workplace meta-analytic evidence supports this.

- **Van den Broeck et al., 2016**, review/meta-analysis: satisfaction of autonomy, competence, and relatedness at work is associated with higher job satisfaction, work engagement, performance, and lower burnout/turnover intentions.
- **Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work: meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions.

This is important because “leading a team” may score high on some intrinsic dimensions: autonomy, impact, mentoring, relatedness, competence growth. The Google role is not necessarily “less intrinsic” just because the technical topic is less interesting.

A good decomposition would be:

- Is the Anthropic role more interesting because of **technical curiosity**?
- Is it also more meaningful?
- Does it give you autonomy?
- Does the Google role give you real ownership, or mainly coordination/admin?
- Would management itself be interesting to you, or do you mainly want the status/career step?

Research would treat those as different variables.

### 5. Scientists and researchers often accept lower pay for research freedom

There is also specific evidence that scientists value “doing science” and research autonomy enough to trade off salary.

- **Stern, 2004, “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”** found evidence of compensating wage differentials: scientists appear willing to accept lower pay for jobs with more science-oriented amenities, such as research freedom or ability to publish.
- Work by **Sauermann and colleagues** on scientists and R&D employees similarly finds that motives like intellectual challenge, independence, contribution to science, and recognition matter substantially alongside pay.

This does not prove those scientists are happier ex post, but it is revealed-preference evidence that for research-oriented people, the non-monetary aspects of research work are valuable enough to substitute for significant compensation.

### 6. What about regret?

There is less direct evidence on “do people regret leaving interesting jobs for higher salary/management?” But regret research gives some clues.

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: the most common life regrets are often in domains like education, career, romance, parenting, and self-improvement. Career is a major regret domain.
- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: in the long run, people often regret inactions more than actions.
- However, that does not mechanically imply “take the Google offer.” Not taking a leadership opportunity could become a regret, but so could leaving rare, highly interesting work.

The regret literature mostly says: people regret choices where they see a salient better counterfactual and where the opportunity felt meaningful. In your case, both sides have regret potential:

- “I had a chance to lead a team and grow as a manager; I passed.”
- “I left uniquely interesting frontier research for a more ordinary job.”

So regret research does not decide the case. It suggests you should reduce uncertainty and preserve optionality.

### Research-grounded takeaway

If the salary increase is modest and you are already financially comfortable, the literature would generally give **more weight to work content, autonomy, meaning, fit, manager/team quality, and growth** than to compensation.

But the Google offer has one big potentially intrinsic feature: **leading a small team**. If that role gives you real autonomy, ownership, mentoring, and a path you deeply want, it may satisfy the same psychological needs as interesting technical work, just differently.

The key empirical question is not:

> Interesting work vs higher salary?

It is more like:

> Which role gives me more of autonomy, competence growth, meaningful impact, identity fit, good collaborators, and sustainable day-to-day energy?

Questions I would ask before deciding:

1. In the Google role, what fraction of my time is technical/research/architecture vs meetings, planning, performance management, coordination?
2. Would I have real roadmap authority, or only responsibility without autonomy?
3. Is the team/project likely to become more interesting once I own it?
4. Am I excited by management itself—coaching, hiring, strategy, alignment—or mainly by the idea of having led a team?
5. Could I get a similar leadership path at Anthropic within 6–18 months?
6. How reversible is the move? Could I return to frontier research if management or the project is not satisfying?
7. After tax and lifestyle effects, how much does the salary difference actually change my freedom/security?

If you wanted the most evidence-consistent heuristic: **do not trade away unusually high intrinsic fit for a small salary increase.** But **do consider trading some technical interestingness for a role that genuinely increases autonomy, impact, learning, and leadership fit**—because those are also among the strongest predictors of long-term work satisfaction.

answer:
Short version: there is no clean study of “stay in the more interesting AI-research job vs take the slightly higher-paid, less interesting team-lead job.” But several adjacent literatures are quite relevant. They mostly say:

1. **Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work is a major predictor of job satisfaction and well-being.**
2. **Salary matters, but above a comfortable baseline its marginal effect is usually smaller than people expect, and smaller than day-to-day job quality.**
3. **Leadership may compensate for lower technical interest if it brings autonomy, status, mentoring, social connection, and a sense of growth.**
4. **Regret research suggests people often regret missed opportunities and failures to live up to “ideal self” goals, but it does not clearly say whether the missed opportunity is “frontier research” or “leading a team.”**

## 1. “Interesting work” is one of the strongest job-satisfaction predictors

The closest academic construct is not exactly “interestingness,” but a cluster: **intrinsic motivation, skill use, autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, person–job fit, and meaningful work**.

A major work-design meta-analysis is:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, *Journal of Applied Psychology*, meta-analysis of work design.

They found that jobs with more **autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and complexity** are reliably associated with higher:

- job satisfaction,
- internal motivation,
- organizational commitment,
- performance,

and lower:

- stress,
- burnout,
- turnover intentions.

This is essentially the research version of “the work itself is interesting and lets me use my abilities.”

Another relevant line is **person–job fit**:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, meta-analysis on person–environment fit.

They found that **person–job fit** is strongly related to job satisfaction and moderately-to-strongly related to commitment and lower intent to quit. In many studies, person–job fit is one of the largest correlates of job satisfaction—often larger than pay level.

So yes: people whose work fits their abilities/interests and feels intrinsically engaging tend to be happier at work.

## 2. Meaningful work also matters a lot

“Interesting” and “meaningful” are distinct but often correlated. If your Anthropic work feels important, socially valuable, or identity-congruent, that is another strong predictor.

Relevant research:

- **Wrzesniewski et al., 1997**: people who see their work as a “calling” report higher work and life satisfaction.
- **Duffy et al., 2018**, meta-analysis on calling: “living a calling” is associated with higher job satisfaction, career commitment, and life satisfaction.
- **Allan et al.**, meta-analytic work on meaningful work: meaningful work is strongly related to job satisfaction and engagement, and negatively related to withdrawal and burnout.

Caveat: “calling” can be double-edged. Studies of calling-oriented workers, e.g. **Bunderson & Thompson, 2009** on zookeepers, find that meaningful work can make people tolerate worse pay, worse conditions, or overwork. So “this work is fascinating/important” is good for well-being only if it does not become a route to chronic stress or self-exploitation.

## 3. Pay matters, but less than many people expect, especially at high income

There is a large literature on income and subjective well-being. The cleanest summary is:

- income is positively related to life satisfaction;
- effects are roughly **logarithmic**, meaning a 10% raise matters more if you earn $50k than if you earn $500k;
- income is more strongly related to evaluative well-being, e.g. “how satisfied am I with my life?”, than to daily experienced happiness;
- the plateau debate is unresolved, but the diminishing-returns point is robust.

Key references:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010**: emotional well-being appeared to plateau around a moderate income level, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth, 2021**: experienced well-being continued rising with income, with no simple plateau.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023**: adversarial collaboration; income keeps helping for many people, but the least-happy subgroup shows more flattening.

For job satisfaction specifically:

- **Judge et al., 2010**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction, found that **absolute pay level has only a modest correlation with job satisfaction**—roughly in the neighborhood of *r* ≈ .15. Pay satisfaction/fairness matters more than raw pay.

Implication for your case: if Google is only “a bit” higher salary and you are already highly paid, the expected marginal happiness gain from salary alone is probably modest. It could still matter if it changes your housing, family security, runway, philanthropy, or perceived fairness—but the research would not usually treat a small raise as a dominant well-being variable.

## 4. Leadership is not just “less interesting work”; it may satisfy other important needs

The Google offer includes leading a small team, which you said you have always wanted to do. Research would treat that as potentially important, because leadership can increase:

- autonomy/control,
- status,
- responsibility,
- mentoring/relatedness,
- perceived impact,
- skill growth,
- identity fulfillment.

Those are all psychologically important.

Relevant lines:

- **Self-Determination Theory** — Deci & Ryan: well-being is strongly linked to autonomy, competence, and relatedness. A team-lead role might score high on all three, even if the technical content is less exciting.
- **Karasek’s job demand–control model** and later research: high demands are less harmful when people have high control. Leadership often increases control, though it also increases demands.
- **Anderson et al., 2012**, “local-ladder” status research: local social status/respect predicts subjective well-being over and above objective socioeconomic status.
- **Sherman et al., 2012**, PNAS: leaders in some samples showed lower cortisol/anxiety, partly because of greater perceived control.

But there is a downside: management can mean more meetings, politics, hiring, performance management, and responsibility without enough authority. Under the **Job Demands–Resources** model, high demands with insufficient resources predict burnout. So the key empirical question is not “management or research?” but:

> Does the team-lead role increase autonomy, competence, relatedness, meaning, and growth more than it increases stress, admin, and loss of intellectual engagement?

## 5. Regret research: people regret missed opportunities, but it won’t tell you which one

There is not much direct research on “do people regret choosing interesting work over money?” But general regret research is relevant.

Important findings:

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**, meta-analysis: people’s biggest life regrets often involve education, career, romance, and family. Career is a major regret domain.
- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: in the short run, people often regret actions; in the long run, they more often regret inactions—things they failed to try.
- **Davidai & Gilovich, 2018**: people tend to regret failures to live up to their “ideal self” more than failures to live up to their “ought self.”

This cuts both ways for you.

You might later regret:

- not taking the chance to lead a team, if leadership is part of your ideal self;
- leaving unusually interesting frontier research, if that is part of your ideal self;
- optimizing for a small salary/title gain if the daily work becomes dull;
- staying in fascinating work if it prevents you from developing the management path you genuinely want.

The regret literature does **not** imply “always take the new opportunity.” It implies that long-term regret is often about **identity-relevant missed opportunities**.

## 6. The most research-backed way to frame your choice

Based on the literature, I would not model this as:

> interesting work vs salary.

I would model it as two bundles of psychological predictors:

### Anthropic role

Score it on:

- intellectual interest,
- meaning/mission,
- autonomy,
- quality of colleagues,
- learning rate,
- work-life sustainability,
- future career optionality,
- likelihood of getting leadership later.

### Google role

Score it on:

- autonomy as team lead,
- actual authority vs responsibility,
- quality of manager/org,
- team quality,
- amount of technical vs admin work,
- whether leadership itself feels intrinsically meaningful,
- future path back to research if desired,
- salary/security/status,
- work-life balance.

The research-weighted takeaway would be:

> If the only Google advantage is modestly higher pay, while the Anthropic role is much more interesting, meaningful, and growth-producing, the evidence generally favors staying with the more intrinsically rewarding work.  
>  
> But if leading a team is a deep goal and the Google role gives real autonomy, skill growth, status, and future optionality, then the “less interesting” role may still be highly satisfying because it satisfies different psychological needs.

The strongest empirical predictors to investigate are probably not salary, but:

1. **Will I have autonomy?**
2. **Will I use and grow valued skills?**
3. **Will the work feel meaningful?**
4. **Will I respect and enjoy the people?**
5. **Will the demands be sustainable?**
6. **Is this aligned with the person I want to become?**
7. **How reversible is the move?**

If Google is only slightly higher pay and materially less interesting on most of those dimensions, research would suggest caution. If it is less technically interesting but much better for autonomy, leadership identity, and long-term career fit, the evidence is more ambiguous.

answer:
Yes — there is quite a lot of relevant research, though not usually framed exactly as “interesting Anthropic research job vs. higher-paid less-interesting Google leadership job.” The closest literatures are work design, intrinsic motivation, person–job fit, pay/job satisfaction, and regret/affective forecasting.

## Short version

The research generally says:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**  
   It tends to matter more for day-to-day happiness than modest differences in salary, especially once income is already high.

2. **Pay matters, but its marginal effect is usually modest at high income levels.**  
   Pay affects security, status, fairness perceptions, and life options, but the correlation between absolute pay and job satisfaction is surprisingly small.

3. **Person–job fit is very important.**  
   People are happier and less likely to quit when their work matches their interests, values, and preferred way of working.

4. **Leadership could itself be an “interesting work” factor.**  
   If leading a small team gives you autonomy, responsibility, mentorship, strategic influence, and growth, it may satisfy many of the same psychological needs as research. If it is mostly coordination, politics, and less intellectual challenge, the literature would predict lower satisfaction.

5. **Direct evidence on long-term regret is thinner.**  
   But broader regret research suggests people often regret forgone self-concordant opportunities — things they genuinely wanted to do — more than forgone extrinsic rewards. That said, career regret is common, and reversibility matters.

---

## 1. Work content is a major predictor of satisfaction

The classic framework is the **Job Characteristics Model** from Hackman and Oldham. It argues that jobs are more motivating and satisfying when they have:

- skill variety,
- task identity,
- task significance,
- autonomy,
- feedback,
- meaningfulness.

This maps closely onto “interesting work.”

Meta-analyses generally support this. For example:

- **Fried & Ferris 1987**, meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model: core job characteristics were reliably related to job satisfaction and internal motivation.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, large meta-analysis of work design: motivational job features such as autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, and complexity showed moderate to large associations with job satisfaction, intrinsic motivation, organizational commitment, and lower turnover intentions.

The practical implication: if “interesting” means intellectually challenging, autonomous, meaningful, and growth-producing, then yes, the evidence suggests it is a very important determinant of happiness at work.

---

## 2. Person–job fit is especially strong

Another relevant literature is **person–environment fit** or **person–job fit**. This asks: does the job match the person’s abilities, interests, needs, and values?

A major meta-analysis:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005**, Personnel Psychology: person–job fit had a strong relationship with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit.

The correlations in this literature are often larger than the correlation between pay and satisfaction. In plain terms: people tend to be much happier when the work itself fits them.

For your situation, the relevant question is not just “Anthropic vs. Google,” but:

> Which job better matches the kind of problems I want to think about every day?

If the Anthropic role is unusually high fit and the Google role is merely “good but less interesting,” that is a serious point in favor of staying.

---

## 3. Pay matters, but less than people often expect

There is a well-known meta-analysis:

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich 2010**, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction”: actual pay level was only weakly correlated with job satisfaction — roughly around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction correlated more with job satisfaction, but still not nearly as strongly as many intrinsic work factors.

This does not mean salary is irrelevant. It matters when:

- you are under financial stress,
- the raise changes your life options materially,
- pay signals respect or fairness,
- you compare yourself to peers,
- compensation affects future career capital.

But if the salary difference is only “a bit higher” and you are already well-compensated, research would generally predict a smaller happiness gain from the pay difference than from a large difference in work content.

On broader income and happiness:

- **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found that emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth 2021** challenged the plateau result, finding experienced well-being continues to rise with income, roughly logarithmically.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** reconciled some of this: for many people, happiness continues to rise with income, but the gains are modest and heterogeneous.

The important point is that at high income levels, the marginal effect of additional salary is usually real but not huge.

---

## 4. Job satisfaction spills over into life satisfaction

Work is a large part of waking life, so job satisfaction matters beyond the office.

Meta-analytic and longitudinal work finds a substantial relationship between job satisfaction and life satisfaction. For example:

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin 1989** found a sizable correlation between job and life satisfaction.
- Later work, e.g. **Bowling, Eschleman & Wang 2010**, also supports a meaningful relationship between job satisfaction and subjective well-being.

Causality is bidirectional: happier people are more likely to be satisfied with their jobs, but jobs also affect happiness. So you should not interpret the correlation as pure causal effect. Still, the association is strong enough that sacrificing day-to-day work quality is not trivial.

---

## 5. Meaningful work and intrinsic motivation are strongly linked to well-being

Self-determination theory, associated with **Deci and Ryan**, argues that people do better when activities satisfy three basic psychological needs:

- autonomy,
- competence,
- relatedness.

Interesting research work often satisfies autonomy and competence. Team leadership may satisfy relatedness, competence, autonomy, and significance — if the role is designed well.

Meta-analytic work on basic psychological needs at work, e.g. **Van den Broeck et al. 2016**, finds that need satisfaction at work is associated with higher engagement and job satisfaction and lower burnout.

There is also a literature on **meaningful work**:

- **Allan et al. 2019**, meta-analysis: meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, work engagement, life satisfaction, and lower withdrawal intentions.

So if your current Anthropic work feels meaningful in addition to interesting, that is a large positive predictor.

---

## 6. Leadership is not just an extrinsic factor

One thing to be careful about: “leading a small team” is not merely a title/status/salary variable. It can change the work along dimensions that research says matter a lot.

A leadership role may give you:

- more autonomy,
- broader task significance,
- mentoring and people development,
- more influence over strategy,
- a new learning curve,
- more feedback and responsibility.

Those are all potentially satisfying.

But it may also bring:

- less deep work,
- more meetings,
- more performance-management burden,
- more politics,
- less technical novelty,
- higher emotional labor,
- more accountability with less control.

The **job demands–resources** literature would say that demanding roles can be energizing when they come with autonomy, support, and resources, but can become draining when demands are high and control/support are low.

So the question is not “research vs. management” abstractly. It is:

> Does this specific Google role provide the job characteristics that predict satisfaction?

If the new role is less intellectually interesting but much higher in autonomy, influence, mentorship, and growth, it could still be satisfying. If it is less interesting and also lower in autonomy/deep work, the evidence would make me cautious.

---

## 7. What about regret?

There is less direct research of the form “people who chose interesting lower-paid jobs later regretted/did not regret it.” But there are relevant findings.

General regret research, e.g.:

- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995**: over the long run, people often regret inactions more than actions.
- **Roese & Summerville 2005**: career and education are among the most common domains of regret.

This suggests that people often regret not pursuing paths that felt important or self-defining. However, this does not clearly favor staying or leaving. You could later regret leaving unusually interesting research, or you could regret not trying leadership when you had the chance.

Affective forecasting research also suggests people can overweight salient, easily measurable factors — money, prestige, title — and underweight day-to-day experience. But again, this is not a universal rule.

---

## My evidence-based interpretation

If I had to summarize the literature in decision-relevant terms:

- A **large difference in work interestingness/meaning/fit** is likely to matter a lot.
- A **small-to-moderate salary increase**, especially from an already high salary, is likely to matter less than people intuitively expect.
- A **leadership opportunity** can matter a lot if it gives autonomy, growth, and meaning.
- The biggest risk is choosing based on the abstract label — “Google,” “higher salary,” “team lead” — rather than the actual day-to-day work.

A useful research-grounded checklist would be to score each role on:

1. Intellectual interest of daily problems  
2. Autonomy  
3. Learning/growth  
4. Meaning/mission  
5. Quality of manager and peers  
6. Ability to do deep work  
7. Social/mentoring satisfaction  
8. Career capital and future optionality  
9. Stress, hours, bureaucracy  
10. Financial/security impact

The literature would generally predict higher happiness from the role that scores higher on autonomy, fit, meaning, competence growth, and daily work quality — unless the salary difference materially changes your life or the leadership role fulfills a strong personal goal.

answer:
There is not much direct research on “leave an unusually interesting research job for a somewhat higher-paying team-lead job,” but several adjacent literatures are quite relevant. The short version is:

> **Day-to-day work content, autonomy, meaning, fit, and relationships are usually much stronger predictors of job satisfaction than incremental pay, especially once pay is already high.**  
> But **leading a team** is not just an extrinsic perk; if it gives autonomy, mastery, identity fulfillment, mentoring, and growth, it may itself be an intrinsically rewarding change.

Below are the research results I’d put most weight on.

---

## 1. “Interesting work” / intrinsic job characteristics are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

Classic work-design research says people are more satisfied and motivated when jobs have:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- task identity,
- feedback,
- intellectual challenge.

This is the Hackman & Oldham “Job Characteristics Model.” Meta-analyses broadly support it.

**Relevant results:**

- **Fried & Ferris 1987**, meta-analysis of the Job Characteristics Model: jobs richer in autonomy, variety, significance, identity, and feedback are reliably associated with higher job satisfaction and internal motivation.
- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson 2007**, large meta-analysis of work design: motivational job characteristics had moderate-to-large correlations with job satisfaction. Autonomy, task significance, skill variety, and feedback tend to matter a lot — often correlations in the ~0.3–0.5 range with satisfaction/engagement.
- “Work itself” is often one of the strongest facets of overall job satisfaction in Job Descriptive Index-type research; pay satisfaction is usually weaker.

So yes: people whose jobs are more intrinsically interesting, autonomous, and meaningful generally report substantially higher job satisfaction.

Causality is not perfectly clean — happier people may also perceive their jobs more positively — but longitudinal and work-redesign studies suggest job design genuinely matters, not just personality.

---

## 2. Person-job fit and interest fit matter a lot

There is a big person-environment fit literature. People are happier when their job matches their interests, abilities, and values.

**Relevant results:**

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson 2005**, meta-analysis: person-job fit is strongly related to job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intention to quit. The correlation between perceived person-job fit and job satisfaction is often around **r ≈ .5**.
- Vocational-interest congruence research — e.g. Holland-style “fit” — finds more modest but reliable associations, often around **r ≈ .2–.3** with satisfaction/performance depending on measurement.

For your case, “interesting research at Anthropic” may represent unusually high person-job fit. If so, that is not a small variable.

---

## 3. Pay matters, but less than people often expect — especially at high income

Pay absolutely matters, particularly when it affects security, debt, housing, family constraints, etc. But the marginal effect of a modest raise is usually much smaller than the effect of work content, autonomy, stress, manager quality, and fit.

**Relevant results:**

- **Judge et al. 2010**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction: actual pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction, about **r ≈ .15**. That explains roughly 2% of the variance in job satisfaction. Pay satisfaction correlates more with job satisfaction than raw pay does, but still not as strongly as intrinsic work factors.
- Income and subjective well-being:  
  - **Kahneman & Deaton 2010** found emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising with log income.  
  - **Killingsworth 2021** found experienced well-being continues to rise with income, roughly log-linearly, with no sharp plateau.  
  - **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers 2023** reconciled some of this: for most people, more income continues to help, but for the least happy group, extra income above a level may not help much.

The important practical point: if you are already highly compensated, a “bit higher” salary probably has a small expected effect on day-to-day happiness unless it changes some real constraint in your life.

A rough intuition from the income-wellbeing literature: a **doubling** of income often has only a small-to-moderate association with well-being; a 10–20% raise is much smaller than that.

---

## 4. Meaningful work is strongly associated with well-being, but can be double-edged

Meaningful work is one of the strongest predictors of engagement and satisfaction.

**Relevant results:**

- **Allan, Batz-Barbarich, Sterling & Tay 2019**, meta-analysis: meaningful work is strongly related to job satisfaction, engagement, organizational commitment, and life satisfaction; negatively related to burnout and withdrawal intentions.
- Self-determination theory research — e.g. **Deci & Ryan**, and workplace meta-analyses like **Van den Broeck et al. 2016** — finds that satisfaction of autonomy, competence, and relatedness needs at work is robustly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, and well-being.

But meaningful/interesting work can also lead people to tolerate worse conditions, overwork, or exploitation. Studies on “calling” — e.g. **Bunderson & Thompson 2009** on zookeepers, and later calling research — find that people with a strong calling often report high meaning but may also accept sacrifices, lower pay, and blurred boundaries.

So “interesting work” is usually good for satisfaction, but it is not automatically good if it comes with unsustainable stress or lack of agency.

---

## 5. Promotions and leadership often increase satisfaction, but also stress

The Google offer is not merely “less interesting work for more money.” It includes leading a small team, which could matter a lot if leadership is a long-standing goal.

Research on promotions and managerial responsibility is mixed but informative.

**Relevant results:**

- Promotions tend to increase job satisfaction, at least initially, because they bring status, pay, autonomy, and perceived progress.
- But there is evidence of a “honeymoon-hangover” pattern after job changes and promotions: satisfaction rises around the transition, then partially fades. See **Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy 2005** on job change and satisfaction.
- **Johnston & Lee 2012** and related work on promotions find that promotions can increase job satisfaction but may also increase stress and time pressure.
- Some studies, e.g. **Boyce & Oswald 2012**, suggest promotions do not necessarily improve mental health and may worsen stress-related outcomes.

Leadership can be satisfying if it gives:

- autonomy,
- influence,
- mentoring,
- scope,
- identity fulfillment,
- learning.

But it can be less satisfying if it mostly means:

- meetings,
- performance management,
- politics,
- less deep work,
- responsibility without authority,
- lower technical/research immersion.

So the research-relevant question is not “leadership good or bad?” It is: **does this specific leadership role increase autonomy, competence, meaning, and fit, or does it mainly substitute people-management overhead for work you currently love?**

---

## 6. Research-specific populations: scientists often trade money for intellectual freedom

There is also a literature on scientists and R&D workers suggesting that intrinsic motivation and intellectual freedom are especially important in research careers.

**Relevant results:**

- **Stern 2004, “Do Scientists Pay to Be Scientists?”** found evidence that scientists accept lower wages to work in environments that allow more scientific freedom, publication, and participation in the broader scientific community. This is revealed-preference evidence: many researchers literally trade pay for more “science-like” work.
- **Sauermann & Cohen 2010** and related work on R&D motivation find that intrinsic motives such as intellectual challenge and desire to contribute to science are important predictors of research behavior and innovation-relevant outcomes.

This seems especially relevant for someone in a research-engineering role at a frontier AI lab. If the Anthropic job has unusually high intellectual/mission fit, the lost nonpecuniary value may be large.

---

## 7. Regret research: direct evidence is limited, but some patterns matter

There is not much direct evidence on “people who stayed in more interesting jobs later regretted not taking higher pay/management.” But regret research has a few robust findings:

- **Roese & Summerville 2005**: education and career are among the most common domains of major life regret.
- **Gilovich & Medvec 1995**: in the short run, people often regret actions; in the long run, they more often regret inactions — things they did not try.
- **Davidai & Gilovich 2018**: people’s most enduring regrets often concern failures to live up to their “ideal self” — their hopes, aspirations, and identity — more than failures of duty.

Applied carefully, this could cut either way:

- If “becoming a leader/manager” is part of your ideal self, not trying it could become a regret.
- If “doing frontier, fascinating AI research” is central to your ideal self, leaving it for a less interesting role could become a regret.
- If the salary difference is only modest, regret research does not suggest that people mainly regret failing to maximize salary, once financial security is adequate.

---

## Evidence-weighted takeaway

If we translate the literature into your situation:

1. **A modest salary increase is probably the weakest reason to move**, assuming you are already financially comfortable.
2. **The interestingness/meaning/fit of the work is likely a major determinant of long-run job satisfaction.**
3. **The leadership opportunity could be genuinely important**, but only if the role gives you autonomy, learning, influence, and a daily schedule you would actually like.
4. **People adapt to title and pay more than to daily work content.** The day-to-day calendar matters enormously.
5. The key empirical question is whether Google’s “less interesting” work is still interesting enough, and whether leading the team compensates intrinsically rather than merely symbolically.

A research-grounded way to evaluate it would be to compare the two jobs on:

- intellectual challenge,
- autonomy,
- meaning/mission,
- learning rate,
- quality of collaborators,
- manager quality,
- time spent in deep work vs meetings,
- stress/load,
- fit with your ideal professional identity,
- future option value.

The literature would generally predict that **day-to-day work content and fit dominate a small compensation difference**, but it would also predict that **a deeply self-concordant leadership opportunity can be worth a lot** if it changes the kind of person/professional you get to become.

answer:
Short version: there is not much research on the exact counterfactual “stay in a fascinating research job vs. take a slightly higher-paid but less interesting leadership job.” But adjacent literatures are fairly consistent:

1. **Intrinsic work qualities — interest, autonomy, mastery, meaning, skill use, learning — are among the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay matters, but above financial comfort, marginal pay increases tend to have smaller effects than day-to-day work quality.**
3. **Leadership could be satisfying if it increases autonomy, impact, mentoring, and identity fit — but not if it mostly replaces interesting work with coordination/admin.**
4. **Regret research suggests people often regret career inactions and missed opportunities, but it does not specifically say “choose the more interesting job.”**

Some relevant results:

---

## 1. Interesting / meaningful / autonomous work is strongly linked to job satisfaction

A large work-design literature going back to **Hackman & Oldham’s Job Characteristics Model** finds that jobs are more satisfying and motivating when they provide:

- skill variety  
- task identity  
- task significance  
- autonomy  
- feedback  
- opportunities for growth and mastery

A major meta-analysis is:

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007, Journal of Applied Psychology**  
They meta-analyzed work design studies and found that motivational job characteristics — especially autonomy, task significance, feedback, variety, and job complexity — have moderate-to-strong relationships with job satisfaction, internal motivation, and engagement.

This is probably the closest academic analogue to “interesting work.” In these studies, the “work itself” is not a minor factor; it is one of the central determinants.

Similarly:

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005, Personnel Psychology**  
Meta-analysis on person–job/person–organization fit. Person–job fit had a strong association with job satisfaction — roughly in the .5 correlation range in many estimates. In plain English: people whose jobs fit their abilities, interests, and needs are much more satisfied.

And:

**Allan et al., 2019, Journal of Management Studies, “Outcomes of Meaningful Work”**  
Meta-analysis showing meaningful work is strongly associated with job satisfaction, engagement, organizational commitment, life meaning, and lower withdrawal intentions.

So the research does support: **people who find their work interesting/meaningful/autonomous tend to be happier at work.** It also spills over into life satisfaction, though less strongly than into job satisfaction.

---

## 2. Pay matters, but marginal pay increases are usually weaker than work-content effects

Pay is not irrelevant. It affects security, status, options, fairness perceptions, family decisions, and life evaluation. But the empirical relationship between pay and job satisfaction is often surprisingly modest.

A key meta-analysis:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010, Journal of Vocational Behavior, “The relationship between pay and job satisfaction”**  
They found that actual pay level correlates only weakly with job satisfaction — around **r ≈ .15**. Pay satisfaction is more related, but still not as strongly as many intrinsic work variables.

On general well-being:

**Kahneman & Deaton, 2010, PNAS** found that emotional well-being increased with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.  
**Killingsworth, 2021, PNAS** found experienced well-being continues to rise with income, but approximately log-linearly — meaning each additional dollar matters less at higher incomes.  
**Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** reconciled these findings: income continues to matter for many people, but the effect is heterogeneous and not infinite.

For your case, “a bit higher salary” at an already high-compensation level is probably not the kind of income change that research would predict to dominate day-to-day work quality — unless the money changes something concrete: financial independence timeline, family constraints, immigration/security, ability to fund projects, etc.

---

## 3. Intrinsic motivation is especially important for creative/research work

For research engineering specifically, intrinsic motivation may matter more than in routine work.

Relevant literature:

**Amabile, 1980s–1990s, componential theory of creativity**  
Creative performance tends to be higher when people are intrinsically motivated by the work itself — curiosity, challenge, intellectual interest — rather than only external rewards.

**Cerasoli, Nicklin & Ford, 2014, Psychological Bulletin**  
Meta-analysis: intrinsic motivation predicts performance, especially performance quality. Extrinsic incentives also matter, especially for quantity, but intrinsic motivation remains important.

So if the Anthropic role is much more intellectually alive for you, that is not just a “nice to have.” For research/creative work, it can affect both happiness and performance.

---

## 4. Job changes often have a “honeymoon–hangover” pattern

There is evidence that people often feel a boost after changing jobs, but the boost can fade.

**Boswell, Boudreau & Tichy, 2005, Journal of Applied Psychology**  
They studied job changers and found a “honeymoon–hangover” effect: satisfaction rises after a job change, then often declines over time.

This matters because salary/title/team-lead novelty may feel very salient now, but the long-run experience may depend more on the recurring daily work: meetings, autonomy, technical depth, colleagues, mission, and learning.

---

## 5. Regret research: career regrets are common, but the evidence is less specific

There is research on life regrets:

**Roese & Summerville, 2005, Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin**  
Meta-analysis found that education and career are among the most common domains of regret.

**Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**  
People tend to regret actions more in the short run, but inactions more in the long run — missed opportunities loom larger over time.

Applied to your case, this cuts both ways:

- You might regret leaving unusually interesting work.
- You might regret not taking a leadership opportunity you “always wanted.”
- Regret tends to be strongest when people feel they had a meaningful, reversible-looking opportunity and did not act.

The regret literature does **not** give a simple answer like “always pick interesting work.” It says missed identity-relevant opportunities can matter a lot.

---

## 6. Leadership is not just “less interesting work” — it may satisfy different intrinsic needs

The Google offer has at least two separable components:

1. **More money/status**
2. **Leading a small team**

The research is skeptical that #1 alone will buy much happiness if you are already financially comfortable.

But #2 may be different. Leadership can increase:

- autonomy
- responsibility
- mentoring
- influence
- task significance
- career capital
- social connection
- identity fulfillment

Those are exactly the kinds of factors that the work-design literature says matter. So the key question is not “interesting work vs. salary.” It is more like:

> Is the Google role less interesting only technically, but more interesting in terms of leadership, impact, ownership, and growth?  
> Or is it genuinely less intrinsically motivating overall?

If leadership is a deep, recurring source of motivation for you, then the Google role may not be “less interesting” in the psychologically relevant sense. It may simply be interesting along a different axis.

---

## My reading of the evidence

If the salary increase is modest and the Anthropic work is substantially more interesting/meaningful, the literature would generally predict that **staying with the more intrinsically motivating work is more likely to preserve job satisfaction**.

However, if leading a small team is a major personal value and the Google role gives real autonomy, mentorship, ownership, and future optionality, then the leadership opportunity could compensate for — or even outweigh — the lower technical interest.

The most research-aligned way to evaluate it would be to ignore title/salary for a moment and compare expected weekly lived experience:

- How much autonomy will I have?
- How much of the work will feel meaningful?
- Will I be learning quickly?
- Will I use my strongest skills?
- Will I work with people I respect?
- Will I get feedback and see progress?
- Will I have enough time for deep work?
- Does the role fit my desired identity: researcher, builder, leader, manager, strategist?

Those variables are much closer to what the job satisfaction literature finds predictive than salary alone.

answer:
Short version: **yes, there is a lot of evidence that “interesting / meaningful / well-fit work” is a major predictor of job satisfaction and well-being**, often stronger than pay level. But there is less direct evidence on the exact counterfactual “stay in the more interesting job vs. take a slightly higher-paid leadership role and later regret it.” The closest literatures are job design, person–job fit, meaningful work, pay satisfaction, promotions, and regret.

## 1. Interesting work / good fit is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction

A large body of industrial-organizational psychology finds that people are more satisfied and engaged when their work has:

- autonomy,
- skill variety,
- task significance,
- task identity,
- feedback,
- complexity / problem-solving,
- meaning,
- fit with interests and values.

This goes back to the **Job Characteristics Model**: Hackman & Oldham, 1976/1980. Later meta-analyses generally support it.

A useful summary is:

**Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, meta-analysis of work design research.  
They found that motivational job characteristics—autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity—are substantially related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and performance. Correlations with job satisfaction are often in the moderate-to-large range, roughly `.3–.5` depending on the characteristic.

Another relevant line:

**Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, meta-analysis of person–job/person–organization fit.  
Person–job fit was strongly associated with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, lower intent to quit, and performance. Subjective person–job fit has correlations with job satisfaction around the `.5` range in many studies.

For vocational interests specifically:

**Nye, Su, Rounds & Drasgow, 2012** and related meta-analyses on vocational interests show that interest fit predicts job performance, satisfaction, and persistence, though objective “interest congruence” effects are often more modest than subjective fit effects.

So the research answer is: **people whose work is more intrinsically interesting, meaningful, autonomous, and well matched to them reliably report higher job satisfaction and lower desire to leave.**

## 2. Meaningful work is also strongly associated with well-being

There is a distinct literature on “meaningful work.”

**Allan et al., 2019**, meta-analysis on meaningful work, found that meaningful work is strongly positively associated with:

- job satisfaction,
- work engagement,
- life satisfaction,
- organizational commitment,

and negatively associated with withdrawal intentions and distress.

The caveat is that much of this literature is correlational. Happier people may perceive work as more meaningful. But the associations are large and consistent, and they line up with experimental/intervention research on job redesign and job crafting.

## 3. Pay matters, but pay level is usually a weaker predictor than people expect

Pay matters more when it affects security, basic comfort, perceived fairness, status, or optionality. But the direct relationship between **absolute pay level** and job satisfaction is often surprisingly weak.

A key reference:

**Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.  
They found that actual pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction—roughly around `r = .15` with overall job satisfaction, somewhat higher with pay satisfaction. In plain English: higher-paid people are somewhat more satisfied, but the relationship is not huge.

This does not mean money is irrelevant. Rather:

- if pay is too low, it matters a lot;
- if pay feels unfair, it matters a lot;
- if the raise changes your life options, it matters;
- but if you are already well paid and the increase is “a bit higher,” the marginal well-being effect may be smaller than the effect of day-to-day work quality.

For broader life satisfaction, the income literature is nuanced:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found emotional well-being rose with income up to a point, while life evaluation kept rising.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** found experienced well-being continued rising with income above that earlier threshold.
- **Kahneman, Killingsworth & Mellers, 2023** reconciled some of this: for many people, well-being keeps increasing with log income, but the effect is not enormous; for the least happy subgroup, there may be a plateau.

For your case, if both jobs pay very well and the difference is moderate, the research would not predict a huge happiness gain from salary alone.

## 4. Promotions / leadership can increase satisfaction, but also stress

Your Google offer is not just “more pay”; it includes **leading a small team**, which is a qualitatively different factor. Research on promotions and managerial responsibility suggests mixed effects.

Some findings:

- Promotions are often associated with higher job satisfaction, partly because they bring status, income, autonomy, and perceived progress.
- But promotions can also increase stress, work hours, responsibility, interpersonal conflict, and administrative load.
- The well-being effect depends a lot on whether the new role increases autonomy and meaning or mostly increases demands.

Relevant examples:

**Kosteas, 2011**, using panel data, found that promotions and expected promotions are associated with higher job satisfaction.

**Johnston & Lee, 2013**, “Extra Status and Extra Stress,” found that promotions can bring status and satisfaction but also more stress, so the overall welfare effect is ambiguous.

In job-design terms, leading a team could be positive if it gives you:

- autonomy,
- mentorship/teaching,
- influence,
- task significance,
- growth,
- identity as a leader.

But it could be negative if it replaces interesting research with:

- coordination,
- meetings,
- performance management,
- roadmap negotiation,
- politics,
- hiring,
- less hands-on technical work.

So the research does not say “management is good” or “management is bad.” It says the relevant question is: **will the leadership role satisfy your intrinsic goals, or is it mostly an extrinsic promotion?**

## 5. Intrinsic goals usually predict well-being better than extrinsic goals

Self-determination theory is relevant here.

**Deci & Ryan** and many subsequent studies distinguish between intrinsic motivations—learning, mastery, autonomy, contribution, relationships—and extrinsic motivations—money, prestige, external approval.

Broadly, pursuit of intrinsic, self-concordant goals is more reliably associated with well-being than pursuit of extrinsic goals. See for example:

- **Sheldon & Elliot, 1999** on self-concordant goals;
- **Kasser & Ryan, 1993/1996** on intrinsic vs extrinsic aspirations;
- later meta-analytic work on materialism, e.g. **Dittmar et al., 2014**, showing materialistic/extrinsic value orientation is generally negatively associated with well-being.

This matters because “leading a team” may be intrinsic or extrinsic depending on why you want it.

If you want leadership because you genuinely enjoy mentoring, coordinating people, owning outcomes, and building a team, it may be a meaningful/intrinsic change. If you want it mainly because it is status, title, or compensation, the evidence would predict a weaker happiness payoff.

## 6. Regret research is less directly applicable, but has two useful lessons

There is not much clean research saying “people regret leaving interesting jobs for higher-paying jobs” or vice versa. But general regret research has some relevant patterns.

**Roese & Summerville, 2005**, meta-analysis/review on life regrets, found that education, career, romance, and parenting are among the most common domains of regret. Career regret is common, but the research does not cleanly specify “interesting work vs salary.”

**Gilovich & Medvec, 1995** found that in the short run people often regret actions, but in the long run they more often regret inactions—things they did not try.

That cuts both ways here:

- You might later regret not trying leadership if that has been a long-standing aspiration.
- You might also regret leaving unusually interesting research if such opportunities are rare.

Affective forecasting research also suggests people are not great at predicting how much future conditions will affect them. People often overweight salient features like salary, prestige, and title, and underweight repeated daily experience: who they interact with, how much autonomy they have, how much of the day is spent on energizing vs draining tasks.

## 7. My research-based synthesis

If we strip away personal opinion, the empirical pattern is roughly:

1. **Interesting, meaningful, autonomous, well-fit work is one of the strongest predictors of job satisfaction.**
2. **Pay matters, but marginal salary increases at already-high income levels usually have smaller effects than people expect.**
3. **Leadership/promotion can increase satisfaction if it brings autonomy, growth, and meaning, but can reduce well-being if it mostly adds stress and less intrinsically rewarding work.**
4. **Regret evidence is indirect: people often regret missed opportunities, especially in career domains, but the research does not clearly favor “stay” or “leave.”**
5. **The most important question is whether the Google role is less interesting overall, or merely less technically/research-interesting but more interesting along leadership/impact dimensions.**

A practical way to map the research to your decision would be to ask:

- Will I spend most days doing work I find intrinsically engaging?
- Will I have more or less autonomy?
- Will I feel the work is meaningful?
- Is the leadership component genuinely attractive to me day-to-day, not just as a title?
- Is the salary difference large enough to materially change my life?
- How rare is my current research opportunity compared with the chance to lead a small team later?
- If I imagine the ordinary Tuesday of each job, which one seems better?

The research would generally advise against over-weighting a modest salary increase, but it would not advise ignoring a genuine leadership aspiration. The key distinction is whether the new role is “less interesting” in a deep daily sense, or just “less aligned with my current research taste but more aligned with my desire to lead.”

answer:
Putting the employer names aside, the research-based answer is roughly:

> For people already earning enough for comfort/security, intrinsic job quality—interesting work, autonomy, mastery, meaning, good colleagues, and fit—usually predicts job satisfaction much more strongly than marginal increases in pay. But “leading a small team” may itself be an intrinsically valuable feature if it gives you autonomy, growth, identity, and a kind of work you’ve wanted to try.

Below are some relevant findings.

---

## 1. Interesting/meaningful work is a major predictor of job satisfaction

A large literature on **job design** finds that people are more satisfied and motivated when their work has:

- skill variety / intellectual challenge  
- autonomy  
- task significance / meaning  
- feedback  
- task identity—owning a coherent piece of work

This goes back to Hackman & Oldham’s **Job Characteristics Model**. Meta-analyses generally find moderate-to-large associations between these characteristics and job satisfaction/motivation.

One useful review/meta-analysis:

- **Humphrey, Nahrgang & Morgeson, 2007**, meta-analysis of work design research.  
  They found that motivational work characteristics—autonomy, task variety, task significance, feedback, complexity—are consistently related to job satisfaction, internal motivation, engagement, and lower turnover intentions. Many correlations are in the approximate **r = .3–.5** range, with autonomy and social support especially important.

Another related body of work is **person–job fit**:

- **Kristof-Brown, Zimmerman & Johnson, 2005**, meta-analysis.  
  Person–job fit is strongly associated with job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and lower intent to quit. The relationship with job satisfaction is often around **r ≈ .5** depending on the fit measure.

So yes: people whose jobs fit their interests/abilities and provide meaningful, autonomous, challenging work tend to be substantially more satisfied.

---

## 2. Pay matters, but less than people often expect—especially after comfort/security

The cleanest comparison is probably pay vs job satisfaction.

- **Judge, Piccolo, Podsakoff, Shaw & Rich, 2010**, meta-analysis on pay and job satisfaction.  
  They found that actual pay level has only a modest relationship with job satisfaction: about **r ≈ .15**. Pay is more related to *pay satisfaction* than to overall job satisfaction, but still not overwhelmingly so.

This does not mean money is irrelevant. It matters a lot when it affects security, debt, housing, family options, or freedom. But once you are already well-paid, a modest raise often has a smaller effect on day-to-day well-being than changes in work content, autonomy, boss/team quality, or workload.

On income and happiness more generally:

- **Kahneman & Deaton, 2010** found that life evaluation rises with income, while emotional well-being appeared to plateau around a comfortable income level.
- **Killingsworth, 2021** challenged the strict plateau result, finding experienced well-being continues to rise with income on a log scale.
- **Killingsworth, Kahneman & Mellers, 2023** reconciled the findings: income continues to matter for many people, but the marginal effect is relatively modest and varies by baseline happiness.

The practical interpretation: a salary increase is real value, but if it is “a bit higher” and you are already financially secure, research would not predict it to dominate large differences in work interest/fit.

---

## 3. Job satisfaction spills over into overall life satisfaction

Work is not isolated from life.

- **Tait, Padgett & Baldwin, 1989**, meta-analysis, found a substantial relationship between job satisfaction and life satisfaction, often cited around **r ≈ .4**.
- Later work generally supports a reciprocal relationship: happier people are more satisfied at work, but better work also contributes to broader well-being.

So “interesting job” is not merely a local preference; it plausibly affects general happiness, mood, identity, and energy outside work.

---

## 4. Intrinsic motivation and autonomy are especially important in creative/research work

For research/engineering work, intrinsic motivation may matter even more than in routine jobs.

Self-determination theory—**Deci & Ryan**; **Gagné & Deci, 2005**—argues that people thrive when work satisfies:

- autonomy  
- competence/mastery  
- relatedness  
- purpose/internalized value

Meta-analytic work on psychological need satisfaction at work finds these needs predict job satisfaction, engagement, performance, and lower burnout.

Also, research on creativity generally finds that intrinsic motivation is important for creative performance. A classic paper:

- **Amabile, 1985/1996**, componential theory of creativity.  
  Intrinsic motivation supports creativity, while controlling extrinsic pressures can sometimes undermine it.

So if the Anthropic role is deeply interesting and gives you high autonomy and frontier-level learning, that is a serious well-being and performance factor, not just “fun.”

---

## 5. “Leading a small team” could be a real positive, not just a status/salary perk

The Google offer has an important non-monetary feature: leadership. The research does not say “interesting IC work always beats management.”

Management/leadership can provide:

- autonomy and influence  
- responsibility  
- mentoring/relatedness  
- status and recognition  
- growth toward a desired identity  
- broader impact

Those are all factors associated with satisfaction.

But leadership also often brings:

- less deep work  
- more meetings  
- interpersonal conflict  
- responsibility without full control  
- performance-management stress  
- context-switching  
- reduced time doing the technical work you originally loved

The **Job Demands–Resources** literature is relevant here: high demands are less harmful when accompanied by resources like autonomy, support, clarity, and control. A leadership role with real decision authority and a good manager can be energizing; a leadership role with high responsibility but low autonomy can be draining.

Evidence on promotions is mixed. Some studies find promotions increase job satisfaction and pay, but they may also increase stress and reduce mental health in some cases. For example:

- **Boyce & Oswald, 2012** found that promotion did not necessarily improve health and could increase mental strain, even though promotions are generally desired.

So the key research-based question is: is “leading a small team” actually giving you autonomy, mastery, and meaning—or mostly admin/status?

---

## 6. Regret research: less direct, but a few relevant patterns

There is not, as far as I know, a clean longitudinal literature saying: “people who chose interesting work over higher pay regretted/did not regret it.” But adjacent regret research says a few useful things.

- **Roese & Summerville, 2005**: people’s biggest regrets often involve education, career, romance, and family—domains with high perceived opportunity.
- **Gilovich & Medvec, 1995**: in the long run, people often regret **inactions** more than actions.
- **Davidai & Gilovich, 2018**: enduring regrets often involve failures to live up to one’s “ideal self,” not just practical mistakes.

Applied to your case, regret could cut either way:

- If “I always wanted to lead a team” is part of your ideal self, declining the Google role may become a regret.
- If “I want to do the most intellectually important research I can” is central to your ideal self, leaving for less interesting work may become a regret.
- If the move is reversible, regret risk is lower. If moving into management makes it hard to return to frontier research, that matters.

---

## 7. A compact way to apply the evidence

I would decompose the choice into research-supported predictors rather than “Anthropic vs Google” or “interest vs money”:

| Factor | Research tendency | Question for you |
|---|---|---|
| Interesting/challenging work | Strong predictor of job satisfaction | How much would day-to-day intellectual engagement drop? |
| Autonomy | Very strong predictor | Which role gives more real decision authority? |
| Mastery/growth | Strong predictor | Are you growing more as a researcher or as a leader? Which matters now? |
| Meaning/impact | Strong predictor | Which work feels more consequential to you? |
| Team/manager quality | Strong predictor | Who would you work with daily? |
| Pay | Positive but often modest after comfort | What concrete life option does the extra money buy? |
| Leadership opportunity | Potentially valuable | Is it real leadership or mostly coordination/admin? |
| Workload/stress | Strong predictor | Will the new role increase meetings, politics, pressure? |
| Reversibility | Important for regret | Can you return to research if management is less satisfying? |

---

## Bottom line from the literature

If the only difference were “much more interesting work” versus “a bit more salary,” the evidence would generally favor the more interesting, better-fitting job—especially at already-high income levels.

But your case is not just salary. The Google role offers leadership, which may satisfy autonomy, growth, status, and a long-standing aspiration. The research-relevant question is therefore:

> Would the leadership role compensate intrinsically for the loss of research interest, or is it mainly a less interesting job with somewhat higher pay and a title?

That distinction matters more than the salary difference.
